terms.definition: A crack or break in rock caused by stress. Unlike faults, fractures (or joints) show no displacement of rock on either side. Fractures control groundwater flow and rock weathering patterns.
terms.example: Columnar jointing (basalt), sheet fractures (granite), regional joint systems
terms.funFact: Devils Tower in Wyoming has perfect columnar fractures from cooling lava - some columns are 2 meters wide and 180 meters tall!

terms.name: Freshwater
terms.hook: The precious 3% of water that sustains all land life
terms.definition: Water with low concentrations of dissolved salts (less than 1,000 ppm). Freshwater makes up only about 2.5% of Earth's water, with most frozen in glaciers. It is essential for drinking, agriculture, and terrestrial ecosystems.
terms.example: Amazon River, Great Lakes, Lake Baikal, groundwater aquifers
terms.funFact: Lake Baikal in Russia contains 20% of the world's unfrozen freshwater - more than all the Great Lakes combined!

terms.name: Fringing Reef
terms.hook: Coral necklaces hugging the shoreline
terms.definition: A coral reef that grows directly from the shore, with no lagoon separating it from land. Fringing reefs are the most common reef type and represent the youngest stage of coral reef development.
terms.example: Red Sea fringing reefs, Hawaii fringing reefs, Caribbean reefs
terms.funFact: Fringing reefs can eventually become barrier reefs if the island they surround slowly sinks - a process Darwin figured out in 1842!

terms.name: Weather Front
terms.hook: The battle lines where air masses clash
terms.definition: The boundary between two air masses of different temperatures and humidity. Fronts are where weather changes occur, bringing clouds, precipitation, and wind shifts as one air mass replaces another.
terms.example: Cold front, warm front, stationary front, occluded front
terms.funFact: Cold fronts move about twice as fast as warm fronts because cold air is denser and pushes under warm air like a wedge!
terms.etymology: Military metaphor adopted by Norwegian meteorologists in 1919

terms.name: Frost
terms.hook: Nature's delicate ice crystals painting the morning
terms.definition: The deposit of ice crystals on surfaces when the temperature drops below freezing and water vapor in the air undergoes deposition. Frost can damage crops and plants by freezing water inside cells.
terms.formation: Clear, calm nights with temperatures below 0°C
terms.example: Hoar frost, ground frost, black frost (kills plants without visible ice)
terms.funFact: Frost forms fastest on clear nights because clouds act like a blanket, trapping heat near the ground!

terms.name: Fumarole
terms.hook: Earth's volcanic steam vents hissing from the depths
terms.definition: An opening in Earth's crust, often near volcanoes, from which hot volcanic gases and steam escape. Fumaroles can reach temperatures over 900°C and deposit colorful mineral deposits around their vents.
terms.formation: Continuous when heat source is active
terms.example: Yellowstone fumaroles, Solfatara (Italy), White Island (New Zealand)
terms.funFact: The sulfur deposits around fumaroles are so valuable that workers at Java's Kawah Ijen volcano risk their lives mining them!

terms.name: Galaxy
terms.hook: Vast island universes of billions of stars
terms.definition: A gravitationally bound system of stars, stellar remnants, gas, dust, and dark matter. Galaxies range from dwarfs with millions of stars to giants with trillions. The Milky Way is our home galaxy.
terms.formation: Billions of years of gravitational collapse
terms.example: Milky Way, Andromeda (M31), Triangulum (M33), Magellanic Clouds
terms.funFact: There are more galaxies in the universe (2 trillion+) than grains of sand on all Earth's beaches!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'galaxias' meaning 'milky' (from the Milky Way)

terms.name: Gentrification
terms.hook: When wealthy renovators transform working-class neighborhoods
terms.definition: The process of renovating and improving a deteriorated urban neighborhood, often resulting in increased property values and the displacement of lower-income residents. Gentrification is controversial for its social impacts.
terms.formation: Years to decades of urban change
terms.example: Brooklyn (New York), Shoreditch (London), Mission District (San Francisco)
terms.funFact: The term was coined in 1964 by sociologist Ruth Glass, describing changes in London where working-class areas became middle-class!

terms.name: Geode
terms.hook: Plain rocks hiding spectacular crystal treasures inside
terms.definition: A hollow rock lined with crystals or mineral matter on the inside, typically formed in volcanic or sedimentary rocks. Geodes form when mineral-rich water enters cavities and precipitates crystals over thousands of years.
terms.example: Keokuk geodes (Iowa), Brazilian amethyst geodes, Mexican geodes
terms.funFact: The world's largest known geode is the Crystal Cave in Ohio - a celestite geode so big you can walk inside it!

terms.name: Geography
terms.hook: The science of understanding Earth and all its places
terms.definition: The study of places and the relationships between people and their environments. Geography examines both physical features (mountains, rivers, climate) and human activities (cities, culture, economics) across Earth's surface.
terms.formation: Ancient discipline dating to Greek scholars
terms.example: Physical geography, human geography, GIS, cartography
terms.funFact: The word 'geography' was first used by Eratosthenes of Cyrene in the 3rd century BCE - he also calculated Earth's circumference with remarkable accuracy!

terms.name: Geologic Time Scale
terms.hook: Earth's 4.5 billion year calendar divided into eons and eras
terms.definition: The system of chronological dating that relates geological strata to time, dividing Earth's history into eons, eras, periods, and epochs. The scale is based on rock sequences and radiometric dating.
terms.formation: Developed over 200 years of geological study
terms.example: Precambrian, Paleozoic, Mesozoic, Cenozoic eras
terms.funFact: If Earth's history were compressed into 24 hours, humans would appear at 11:58:43 PM - we've been here for just 77 seconds!

terms.name: Geomorphology
terms.hook: The science of how Earth's surface gets its shape
terms.definition: The study of landforms and the processes that shape them. Geomorphology examines how erosion, deposition, tectonics, and weathering create and modify Earth's surface features over time.
terms.example: Fluvial geomorphology, glacial geomorphology, coastal geomorphology
terms.funFact: Geomorphologists can read a landscape like a book - every hill, valley, and plain tells a story of the forces that shaped it!

terms.name: Geosphere
terms.hook: All the solid rock from surface to core
terms.definition: The solid parts of Earth, including the crust, mantle, and core. The geosphere interacts with the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and biosphere through processes like volcanism, erosion, and the rock cycle.
terms.example: Crust, mantle, outer core, inner core
terms.funFact: The geosphere weighs about 6 × 10^24 kg - that's 6,000,000,000,000,000,000,000,000 kilograms of rock and metal!

terms.name: Geothermal
terms.hook: Earth's internal heat escaping to warm the surface
terms.definition: Relating to the internal heat of Earth. Geothermal energy harnesses heat from the planet's interior through hot springs, geysers, and deep wells. Geothermal gradients measure how temperature increases with depth.
terms.example: Iceland geothermal plants, Yellowstone, New Zealand geothermal fields
terms.funFact: Iceland heats 90% of its homes with geothermal energy - they literally warm their houses with volcanic heat!

terms.name: Geyser
terms.hook: Underground pressure cookers erupting in spectacular fountains
terms.definition: A hot spring that periodically erupts, shooting water and steam into the air. Geysers require a heat source, water supply, and specific plumbing geometry to build pressure and release it explosively.
terms.formation: Requires rare combination of geological conditions
terms.example: Old Faithful (Yellowstone), Strokkur (Iceland), El Tatio (Chile)
terms.funFact: There are only about 1,000 geysers on Earth, with half of them in Yellowstone - the largest concentration anywhere!
terms.etymology: From Icelandic 'Geysir', a famous geyser, from 'geysa' (to gush)

terms.name: Glacier
terms.hook: Rivers of ice flowing in slow-motion majesty
terms.definition: A persistent body of dense ice that forms where snow accumulation exceeds melting over many years. Glaciers flow under their own weight, carving landscapes and storing about 69% of Earth's fresh water.
terms.example: Antarctic ice sheet, Greenland ice sheet, Himalayan glaciers, Alpine glaciers
terms.funFact: If all glaciers melted, sea levels would rise about 70 meters - that would put New York City under 60+ meters of water!

terms.name: Gneiss
terms.hook: Ancient rocks banded by heat and pressure's embrace
terms.definition: A high-grade metamorphic rock with distinctive banding of light and dark minerals. Gneiss forms deep in the crust where intense heat and pressure reorganize minerals into parallel bands.
terms.example: Acasta Gneiss (oldest rock, 4.03 billion years), Lewisian Gneiss, Morton Gneiss
terms.funFact: The Acasta Gneiss in Canada is 4.03 billion years old - it formed when Earth was just 500 million years old!

terms.name: Gondwana
terms.hook: The ancient southern supercontinent that birthed many lands
terms.definition: A supercontinent that existed from about 550 to 180 million years ago, comprising present-day South America, Africa, Antarctica, Australia, and India. Gondwana was the southern portion of the larger supercontinent Pangaea.
terms.example: Modern continents that were once part of Gondwana
terms.funFact: You can find identical fossils of Glossopteris ferns on all former Gondwanan continents - proof they were once connected!
terms.etymology: Named after the Gond people of central India

terms.name: Gorge
terms.hook: Deep rocky channels where rivers cut through mountains
terms.definition: A narrow, deep valley with steep, rocky sides, typically carved by a river through resistant rock. Gorges are similar to canyons but often more narrow and with more vertical walls.
terms.example: Columbia River Gorge, Yangtze Gorges, Verdon Gorge (France)
terms.funFact: The Tiger Leaping Gorge in China is so narrow that legend says a tiger escaped hunters by leaping across it!

terms.name: Graben
terms.hook: A block of earth dropped down between parallel faults
terms.definition: A depressed block of land bordered by parallel faults, formed when the crust is stretched and pulled apart. Grabens create rift valleys and are often associated with volcanic activity.
terms.example: East African Rift, Rhine Graben, Basin and Range Province
terms.funFact: Death Valley is a graben - it's actually the block of earth between two faults that dropped down as the Sierra Nevada rose up!

terms.name: Gradient
terms.hook: How steeply the land slopes downhill
terms.definition: The rate of change of elevation over horizontal distance, expressed as a ratio, percentage, or angle. Stream gradient controls water velocity and erosive power; steeper gradients mean faster flow.
terms.example: River gradient, road grade, slope gradient
terms.funFact: The steepest street in the world is Baldwin Street in New Zealand with a 35% gradient - for every 3 meters forward, you climb 1 meter up!

terms.name: Granite
terms.hook: The tough, speckled rock that forms continents' foundations
terms.definition: A coarse-grained igneous rock composed mainly of quartz and feldspar, with some mica. Granite forms from slowly cooling magma deep underground and is the most common plutonic rock in Earth's crust.
terms.formation: Millions of years of slow cooling
terms.example: Yosemite granite, Mount Rushmore, Stone Mountain
terms.funFact: Mount Rushmore was carved in granite because it erodes only 1 inch every 10,000 years - the faces should last at least 7 million years!

terms.name: Grassland
terms.hook: Vast seas of waving grass supporting great herds
terms.definition: A biome dominated by grasses where trees are rare due to insufficient rainfall, seasonal drought, or fire. Grasslands cover about 40% of Earth's land surface and include prairies, steppes, savannas, and pampas.
terms.formation: Climate and fire ecology over millennia
terms.example: North American prairies, African savanna, Eurasian steppe, Argentine pampas
terms.funFact: Grassland soils are among the most fertile on Earth - that's why most grasslands have been converted to farmland!

terms.name: Gravitational Waves
terms.hook: Ripples in spacetime from cosmic collisions
terms.definition: Disturbances in the fabric of spacetime generated by accelerated masses, predicted by Einstein in 1916 and first detected in 2015. Gravitational waves travel at the speed of light and carry information about their sources.
terms.formation: Massive accelerating objects (merging black holes, neutron stars)
terms.example: Black hole mergers, neutron star collisions, supernovae
terms.funFact: The first gravitational waves detected came from two black holes merging 1.3 billion light-years away - the collision converted 3 solar masses into pure energy!

terms.name: Gravity
terms.hook: The force that keeps our feet on Earth and planets in orbit
terms.definition: The fundamental force of attraction between objects with mass. On Earth, gravity gives objects weight and causes falling. In space, gravity governs planetary orbits, star formation, and galaxy structure.
terms.example: Earth's gravity (9.8 m/s²), Moon's gravity (1.6 m/s²), black hole gravity
terms.funFact: You weigh less on a mountaintop than at sea level because you're slightly farther from Earth's center!

terms.name: Greenhouse Effect
terms.hook: Atmospheric gases trapping heat like a planetary blanket
terms.definition: The warming of Earth's surface due to atmospheric gases (water vapor, CO2, methane) absorbing and re-emitting infrared radiation. The natural greenhouse effect keeps Earth habitable; enhanced greenhouse effect causes global warming.
terms.formation: Natural atmospheric process, enhanced by emissions
terms.example: Water vapor effect, CO2 warming, methane from wetlands
terms.funFact: Without the natural greenhouse effect, Earth's average temperature would be -18°C instead of +15°C - too cold for liquid water!
terms.etymology: Named after glass greenhouses that trap heat similarly

terms.name: Prime Meridian
terms.hook: The imaginary line where east meets west at zero degrees
terms.definition: The meridian at 0° longitude, passing through the Royal Observatory in Greenwich, England. The Prime Meridian divides Earth into Eastern and Western Hemispheres and serves as the basis for time zones.
terms.location: From pole to pole through Greenwich
terms.example: Greenwich, England; Ghana; Algeria; Antarctica
terms.funFact: Before 1884, there were multiple prime meridians - Paris, Rome, and others all claimed 0° longitude for themselves!

terms.name: Groundwater
terms.hook: Hidden reservoirs of water filling spaces in rock and soil
terms.definition: Water located beneath Earth's surface in soil pores and rock fractures. Groundwater supplies wells, springs, and streams, and accounts for about 30% of Earth's freshwater (excluding ice).
terms.formation: Infiltration and accumulation over years to millennia
terms.example: Ogallala Aquifer, artesian wells, springs
terms.funFact: Some groundwater in deep aquifers is over a million years old - older than our entire species!

terms.name: Gulf
terms.hook: The ocean's deep reaches extending into the land
terms.definition: A large inlet of a sea or ocean partially enclosed by land, larger and more deeply indented than a bay. Gulfs often have significant economic importance for shipping and fisheries.
terms.formation: Tectonic activity, sea level changes, or erosion
terms.example: Gulf of Mexico, Persian Gulf, Gulf of California
terms.funFact: The Gulf Stream carries more water than all the world's rivers combined - it's an ocean river flowing through the Atlantic!

terms.name: Gully
terms.hook: Rain carving deep scars into unprotected hillsides
terms.definition: A small, steep-sided channel cut into soil or weak rock by concentrated runoff water. Gullies are larger than rills but smaller than ravines and indicate serious soil erosion problems.
terms.formation: Rapid erosion from concentrated water flow
terms.example: Agricultural gullies, badland gullies, construction site erosion
terms.funFact: A single storm can create gullies meters deep in unprotected soil - topsoil that took thousands of years to form can be lost in hours!

terms.name: Gyre
terms.hook: Massive spinning current systems that circle the oceans
terms.definition: A large system of circular ocean currents formed by global wind patterns and the Coriolis effect. There are five major oceanic gyres, each rotating clockwise or counterclockwise depending on hemisphere.
terms.example: North Atlantic Gyre, North Pacific Gyre, Indian Ocean Gyre
terms.funFact: The Great Pacific Garbage Patch accumulates in the North Pacific Gyre because the rotating currents trap floating debris!

terms.name: Habitat
terms.hook: The address where a species makes its living
terms.definition: The natural environment in which an organism or population lives, including all living and non-living factors or conditions. Habitats provide food, shelter, water, and space that species need to survive.
terms.example: Coral reef habitat, forest canopy, tidal zone, cave habitat
terms.funFact: Some species have incredibly specific habitat requirements - the Karner blue butterfly only lives where wild lupine grows!

terms.name: Hail
terms.hook: Ice balls grown in violent thunderstorm updrafts
terms.definition: Balls or lumps of ice (hailstones) that fall from cumulonimbus clouds during severe thunderstorms. Hailstones form when supercooled water droplets accumulate on ice pellets cycling through the cloud.
terms.example: Hailstorms in Great Plains (USA), Argentina, Bangladesh
terms.funFact: The largest hailstone ever recorded fell in Vivian, South Dakota in 2010 - it was 20 cm in diameter and weighed almost 1 kg!

terms.name: Hanging Valley
terms.hook: Smaller valleys left stranded high above the main valley floor
terms.definition: A tributary valley that enters a main valley from a higher elevation, often with a waterfall at the junction. Hanging valleys form when a smaller glacier carves a shallower valley than the main glacier.
terms.example: Yosemite Valley tributaries, Lauterbrunnen Valley (Switzerland), Norwegian fjords
terms.funFact: Yosemite Falls plunges from a hanging valley - the spectacular 739-meter drop exists because a smaller glacier couldn't carve as deep as the main one!
terms.etymology: English descriptive term for the landform

terms.name: Hardpan
terms.hook: A cement-like layer blocking water and roots underground
terms.definition: A dense, impermeable layer in soil, often rich in calcium carbonate, silica, or iron oxite. Hardpan prevents water infiltration and root penetration, forming through long-term soil processes or compaction.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of mineral accumulation
terms.example: Caliche (Southwest USA), laterite hardpan, plow pan (human-caused)
terms.funFact: Some hardpan layers are so tough that farmers have to dynamite them before they can plant deep-rooted crops!

terms.name: Harmattan
terms.hook: The dusty desert wind sweeping across West Africa
terms.definition: A dry, dusty northeasterly trade wind that blows from the Sahara Desert across West Africa from November to March. The Harmattan carries fine dust that reduces visibility and dries out vegetation.
terms.example: Nigeria, Ghana, Senegal, Mali
terms.funFact: Harmattan dust is so fine that it can travel across the Atlantic Ocean and fertilize the Amazon rainforest!

terms.name: Headland
terms.hook: Rocky promontories standing firm against ocean assault
terms.definition: A narrow piece of land jutting out into a body of water. Headlands are typically composed of harder rock that resists erosion better than the surrounding coastline, often creating dramatic cliffs.
terms.example: Land's End (England), Point Reyes (California), Cape Point (South Africa)
terms.funFact: Headlands often become islands eventually - continual erosion can cut through the neck connecting them to the mainland!

terms.name: Headwaters
terms.hook: Where great rivers begin as humble springs and streams
terms.definition: The source or sources of a river, typically located in highlands or mountains. Headwaters include the small streams and springs that feed into the main river channel at its uppermost reaches.
terms.example: Mississippi headwaters (Lake Itasca), Amazon headwaters (Andes), Nile headwaters
terms.funFact: The true source of the Amazon was only definitively determined in 2014 - a glacial stream on Mount Mismi in Peru!

terms.name: Urban Heat Island
terms.hook: Cities creating their own hot zones amid cooler countryside
terms.definition: An urban area that is significantly warmer than its surrounding rural areas due to human activities, dark surfaces absorbing heat, reduced vegetation, and waste heat from buildings and vehicles.
terms.example: Tokyo, New York City, Phoenix, Mumbai
terms.funFact: Large cities can be 1-3°C warmer than surrounding areas during the day and up to 12°C warmer at night!
terms.etymology: English descriptive term coined mid-20th century

terms.name: Hemisphere
terms.hook: Halving the globe by equator or prime meridian
terms.definition: Half of the Earth, divided by either the equator (Northern and Southern Hemispheres) or the Prime Meridian (Eastern and Western Hemispheres). Hemispheres have distinct seasonal patterns and geographic distributions.
terms.example: Northern Hemisphere, Southern Hemisphere, Eastern Hemisphere
terms.funFact: About 90% of the world's population lives in the Northern Hemisphere - the south is dominated by ocean!

terms.name: Hill
terms.hook: Gentle rises in the land too modest to be mountains
terms.definition: A naturally elevated landform lower and less steep than a mountain. While there's no official distinction, hills are generally considered to be less than 600 meters above surrounding terrain.
terms.example: Seven Hills of Rome, Chocolate Hills (Philippines), Scottish Highlands
terms.funFact: The Chocolate Hills of the Philippines include 1,268 nearly identical grass-covered hills - their origin is still debated by geologists!

terms.name: Himalayas
terms.hook: Earth's rooftop where two continents collided
terms.definition: The highest mountain range on Earth, stretching 2,400 km across five countries. The Himalayas formed from the collision of the Indian and Eurasian tectonic plates and contain 10 of the 14 peaks over 8,000 meters.
terms.location: South Asia (Nepal, Tibet, India, Pakistan, Bhutan)
terms.example: Mount Everest (8,849m), K2, Kangchenjunga, Annapurna
terms.funFact: The Himalayas are still rising about 5 mm per year as India continues pushing into Asia!

terms.name: Hoodoo
terms.hook: Stone pillars wearing harder rock caps like hats
terms.definition: A tall, thin spire of rock formed by erosion, typically found in arid environments. Hoodoos develop when a harder cap rock protects softer underlying rock from erosion, leaving a pillar.
terms.formation: Differential erosion over millions of years
terms.example: Bryce Canyon (Utah), Cappadocia (Turkey), Drumheller (Canada)
terms.funFact: Native American Paiute legend says hoodoos are the Legend People, turned to stone for their bad deeds!
terms.etymology: Possibly from voodoo or American frontier slang

terms.name: Horizon
terms.hook: Where Earth and sky appear to meet in the distance
terms.definition: The apparent line where Earth's surface and sky meet, or in soil science, a distinct layer of soil with specific characteristics. The visible horizon's distance depends on observer height and atmospheric conditions.
terms.example: Sea horizon, A horizon (topsoil), B horizon (subsoil)
terms.funFact: At sea level, the horizon is only about 5 km away - on Mount Everest's summit, you can see 336 km!

terms.name: Glacial Horn
terms.hook: A pointed peak sculptured by glaciers from all sides
terms.definition: A sharp, pointed mountain peak formed by glacial erosion from multiple sides. Horns develop when three or more cirques erode back toward each other, leaving a dramatic pyramid-shaped peak.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of cirque erosion
terms.example: Matterhorn (Switzerland), Mount Assiniboine (Canada), Grand Teton
terms.funFact: The Matterhorn's iconic shape was carved by four glaciers eroding from different directions - that's why it looks like a perfect pyramid!
terms.etymology: From the Matterhorn's shape resembling a horn

terms.name: Horst
terms.hook: A block of earth standing proud between sunken neighbors
terms.definition: An elevated block of land between two parallel faults, formed when surrounding blocks drop down during crustal extension. Horsts are the opposite of grabens and often form mountain ranges in rift systems.
terms.example: Black Forest (Germany), Vosges Mountains, Sierra Nevada
terms.funFact: The Black Forest in Germany is a horst - it stands high while the Rhine Graben sank down beside it!

terms.name: Hotspot
terms.hook: Mantle plumes burning holes through moving plates
terms.definition: A volcanic region thought to be fed by underlying mantle plumes, producing a chain of volcanoes as a tectonic plate moves over the stationary hotspot. Hotspots create volcanic island chains and flood basalt provinces.
terms.example: Hawaii hotspot, Yellowstone hotspot, Iceland hotspot, Galápagos
terms.funFact: The Hawaiian islands are getting progressively older to the northwest - Kauai is 5 million years old while the Big Island is still forming!

terms.name: Hot Spring
terms.hook: Where underground heat meets groundwater at the surface
terms.definition: A spring of naturally heated water, typically heated by geothermal energy. Hot springs occur where hot water rises from depth without erupting like a geyser, often depositing colorful minerals.
terms.formation: Groundwater heated by magma or hot rock
terms.example: Grand Prismatic Spring (Yellowstone), Blue Lagoon (Iceland), Beppu (Japan)
terms.funFact: The Grand Prismatic Spring's rainbow colors come from different heat-loving bacteria living at different temperatures!

terms.name: Humidity
terms.hook: The invisible water vapor making air feel thick or thin
terms.definition: The amount of water vapor present in the air. Relative humidity expresses how much water vapor is present compared to the maximum possible at that temperature, measured as a percentage.
terms.example: Tropical humidity (~80%), desert humidity (~20%), comfortable indoor (~50%)
terms.funFact: High humidity makes hot days feel hotter because sweat doesn't evaporate well - that's why 30°C feels worse in Miami than Phoenix!

terms.name: Humus
terms.hook: Decomposed organic matter giving soil its dark richness
terms.definition: The stable, dark organic component of soil formed by decomposition of plant and animal matter. Humus improves soil structure, water retention, and nutrient availability, making it essential for plant growth.
terms.example: Forest floor humus, compost, prairie soil
terms.funFact: It takes about 500 years to form 2.5 cm of humus-rich topsoil naturally - treat your soil gently!

terms.name: Hurricane
terms.hook: Ocean-born heat engines of destructive rotating fury
terms.definition: A severe tropical cyclone with sustained winds of 119 km/h (74 mph) or greater, occurring in the Atlantic Ocean and northeastern Pacific. Hurricanes draw energy from warm ocean water and can cause catastrophic damage.
terms.formation: Days over warm tropical ocean water
terms.example: Hurricane Katrina (2005), Hurricane Maria (2017), Hurricane Dorian (2019)
terms.funFact: A single hurricane can release 600 trillion watts of energy - equivalent to 10,000 nuclear bombs!

terms.name: Hydrological Cycle
terms.hook: Water's endless journey from ocean to sky to land and back
terms.definition: The continuous movement of water through Earth's systems via evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, and runoff. The water cycle drives weather, shapes landscapes, and supports all life.
terms.example: Ocean evaporation, rainfall, river flow, groundwater
terms.funFact: The water you drink today has been recycled countless times - a molecule in your glass may have been part of a dinosaur's blood!

terms.name: Hydrosphere
terms.hook: All the water on Earth from ocean depths to atmosphere
terms.definition: All the water on Earth's surface, underground, and in the atmosphere. The hydrosphere includes oceans (97%), ice (2%), fresh surface water, groundwater, and atmospheric water vapor.
terms.example: Oceans, glaciers, lakes, rivers, groundwater, water vapor
terms.funFact: Earth's water is incredibly ancient - most of it arrived on comets and asteroids over 4 billion years ago!

terms.name: Hypocenter (Focus)
terms.hook: The underground point where earthquakes truly begin
terms.definition: The point within Earth where an earthquake rupture starts, located beneath the epicenter. Earthquake depth (shallow, intermediate, or deep) is measured from the surface to the hypocenter.
terms.location: Within Earth's crust or upper mantle
terms.example: 2011 Japan earthquake (29 km deep), deep South American earthquakes (600+ km)
terms.funFact: The deepest earthquakes occur about 700 km down - any deeper and rock flows instead of breaking!

terms.name: Ice Age
terms.hook: When glaciers conquered continents for thousands of years
terms.definition: A period of long-term reduction in Earth's temperature, resulting in expansion of continental and polar ice sheets and glaciers. The most recent ice age ended about 11,700 years ago; we are technically in an interglacial period.
terms.formation: Tens of thousands to millions of years
terms.example: Last Glacial Maximum (26,000 years ago), Pleistocene ice ages
terms.funFact: During the last ice age, sea levels were 120 meters lower - you could walk from England to France and Russia to Alaska!
terms.etymology: English term coined in the 19th century

terms.name: Ice Cap
terms.hook: Dome-shaped glaciers covering highland peaks
terms.definition: A dome-shaped glacier covering less than 50,000 km², typically found on highlands. Ice caps flow outward from their centers and are smaller than ice sheets but larger than alpine glaciers.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of snow accumulation
terms.example: Vatnajökull (Iceland), Devon Ice Cap (Canada), Quelccaya Ice Cap (Peru)
terms.funFact: Iceland's Vatnajökull ice cap hides several active volcanoes beneath it - eruptions can cause catastrophic glacial floods!

terms.name: Ice Sheet
terms.hook: Continental glaciers burying landscapes miles deep in ice
terms.definition: A mass of glacial ice covering more than 50,000 km², also called continental glaciers. Currently, Earth has two ice sheets: Antarctic (14 million km²) and Greenland (1.7 million km²).
terms.example: Antarctic Ice Sheet, Greenland Ice Sheet
terms.funFact: The Antarctic ice sheet is so heavy it has pushed the bedrock beneath it 500 meters below sea level!

terms.name: Iceberg
terms.hook: Floating ice mountains with most of their mass hidden below
terms.definition: A large piece of freshwater ice broken off from a glacier or ice shelf, floating in open water. Only about 10% of an iceberg is visible above the waterline due to ice being slightly less dense than water.
terms.formation: Calving from glaciers and ice shelves
terms.example: Antarctic tabular icebergs, North Atlantic icebergs, A-68 iceberg (5,800 km²)
terms.funFact: The iceberg that sank the Titanic was relatively small at about 125 feet tall - Antarctica produces icebergs bigger than small countries!

terms.name: Igneous Rock
terms.hook: Rocks born from fire and molten Earth
terms.definition: Rock formed by the cooling and solidification of magma or lava. Igneous rocks are classified as intrusive (cooled slowly underground) or extrusive (cooled quickly at the surface) based on their formation.
terms.example: Granite (intrusive), basalt (extrusive), obsidian, pumice
terms.funFact: All rock on Earth was originally igneous - the first rocks formed from the cooling of the early magma ocean!

terms.name: Infiltration
terms.hook: Rain water soaking down through the soil
terms.definition: The process by which water on the ground surface enters the soil. Infiltration rate depends on soil type, vegetation, moisture content, and slope. Water that infiltrates may become groundwater or soil moisture.
terms.example: Rainfall infiltration, irrigation, snowmelt absorption
terms.funFact: Sandy soils can infiltrate over 200 mm of water per hour, while clay soils may only absorb 1-5 mm!

terms.name: Inlet
terms.hook: Where the sea reaches narrow fingers into the land
terms.definition: A narrow water passage between land masses, connecting a larger body of water to an interior bay, lagoon, or river mouth. Inlets are important for navigation and tidal exchange.
terms.formation: Erosion, sea level rise, or tectonic activity
terms.example: Christchurch Inlet (New Zealand), Murrells Inlet (USA), tidal inlets
terms.funFact: Barrier island inlets can migrate kilometers over decades as sand shifts - some have completely closed while new ones open!

terms.name: Inselberg
terms.hook: Island mountains rising abruptly from surrounding plains
terms.definition: An isolated rocky hill or mountain rising dramatically from a relatively flat plain, formed by erosion leaving resistant rock exposed. Inselbergs are common in tropical regions with deep weathering.
terms.formation: Millions of years of differential erosion
terms.example: Uluru (Australia), Sugarloaf Mountain (Brazil), Spitzkoppe (Namibia)
terms.funFact: Inselbergs often have unique ecosystems on their summits - isolated for millions of years, they're like ecological islands!

terms.name: Insolation
terms.hook: The solar energy received on Earth's surface
terms.definition: The amount of solar radiation reaching a given area, varying with latitude, season, time of day, cloud cover, and atmospheric conditions. Insolation drives climate, weather, and photosynthesis.
terms.example: Equatorial maximum, polar minimum, seasonal variation
terms.funFact: The Sahara Desert receives enough solar energy in 6 hours to power the entire world for a year - if we could capture it!

terms.name: Interglacial
terms.hook: Warm spells between the frozen grip of ice ages
terms.definition: A warmer period between glacial advances when ice sheets retreat. We currently live in the Holocene interglacial, which began about 11,700 years ago. Interglacials typically last 10,000-30,000 years.
terms.example: Holocene (current), Eemian (130,000-115,000 years ago)
terms.funFact: During the last interglacial (Eemian), hippos lived in England and sea levels were 6-9 meters higher than today!

terms.name: International Date Line
terms.hook: The invisible line where tomorrow becomes today
terms.definition: An imaginary line running roughly along the 180° meridian in the Pacific Ocean, where the date changes by one day when crossed. Traveling westward adds a day; traveling eastward subtracts a day.
terms.example: Crossing between Fiji and Samoa, Alaska-Russia boundary
terms.funFact: When it's Monday in Tokyo, it's still Sunday in Honolulu - the International Date Line makes time travel possible (sort of)!
terms.etymology: English descriptive term for date boundary

terms.name: Intertidal Zone
terms.hook: The twice-daily drowned and exposed world between tides
terms.definition: The area of shoreline between high and low tide marks, experiencing regular exposure to air and submersion in water. The intertidal zone hosts uniquely adapted organisms tolerating dramatic environmental changes.
terms.example: Rocky shores, tide pools, mudflats, mangroves
terms.funFact: Intertidal organisms must survive being baked by sun, frozen in winter, pounded by waves, and submerged in salt water - all in one day!

terms.name: Intertropical Convergence Zone
terms.hook: Where trade winds meet and thunderstorms are born
terms.definition: A belt of low pressure circling Earth near the equator where northeast and southeast trade winds converge. The ITCZ is characterized by rising air, heavy precipitation, and frequent thunderstorms.
terms.example: Monsoon rains in India, West African rainfall, Amazon wet season
terms.funFact: The ITCZ moves north and south with the seasons - its migration controls monsoons that feed billions of people!

terms.name: Intrusive Rock
terms.hook: Magma that cooled slowly in darkness below ground
terms.definition: Igneous rock formed from magma that cooled slowly beneath Earth's surface, allowing large crystals to form. Intrusive rocks are exposed at the surface only after overlying rocks erode away.
terms.formation: Millions of years of slow cooling
terms.example: Granite, diorite, gabbro, pegmatite
terms.funFact: The Sierra Nevada batholith took millions of years to cool - that's why its granite has such large, visible crystals!

terms.name: Ionosphere
terms.hook: The electrified layer that bounces radio waves around Earth
terms.definition: A region of Earth's upper atmosphere (60-1000 km) containing electrically charged particles (ions) created by solar radiation. The ionosphere reflects certain radio waves and creates auroras.
terms.example: Radio propagation, GPS signals, aurora formation
terms.funFact: Before satellites, long-distance radio worked by bouncing signals off the ionosphere - a natural reflector in the sky!

terms.name: Island
terms.hook: Land completely surrounded by water's embrace
terms.definition: A naturally formed area of land surrounded by water on all sides and remaining above water at high tide. Islands form through volcanic activity, tectonic processes, sediment deposition, or rising sea levels.
terms.location: Oceans, seas, lakes, and rivers worldwide
terms.example: Greenland (largest), Madagascar, Japan, Hawaiian Islands
terms.funFact: Indonesia has over 17,500 islands, more than any other country - but only about 6,000 are inhabited!

terms.name: Island Arc
terms.hook: Volcanic chains born where ocean plates dive under
terms.definition: A curved chain of volcanic islands formed parallel to an oceanic trench where one tectonic plate subducts beneath another. Island arcs are characterized by earthquakes, volcanism, and mountain building.
terms.formation: Millions of years of volcanic activity
terms.example: Japanese archipelago, Aleutian Islands, Lesser Antilles, Mariana Islands
terms.funFact: Japan sits on four tectonic plates, making it one of the most earthquake-prone and volcanically active places on Earth!
terms.etymology: English descriptive term combining shape with formation

terms.name: Isobar
terms.hook: Lines connecting equal pressure on weather maps
terms.definition: A line on a weather map connecting points of equal atmospheric pressure at a given time. Closely spaced isobars indicate strong pressure gradients and high winds; widely spaced isobars indicate calm conditions.
terms.example: Tightly packed around hurricanes, widely spaced in high pressure
terms.funFact: Wind blows approximately parallel to isobars in the upper atmosphere, but crosses them at an angle near the surface due to friction!

terms.name: Isotherm
terms.hook: Lines tracing equal temperatures across maps
terms.definition: A line on a map connecting points having the same temperature at a given time or for a given period. Isotherms reveal temperature patterns affected by latitude, altitude, ocean currents, and landmasses.
terms.example: July 20°C isotherm, January freezing line
terms.funFact: Isotherms bend toward the equator over continents in winter and toward the poles in summer - showing land heats and cools faster than oceans!

terms.name: Isostasy
terms.hook: Earth's crust floating and bobbing on the mantle below
terms.definition: The gravitational equilibrium between Earth's crust and mantle, where the crust 'floats' on the denser mantle like ice on water. Changes in surface load (ice sheets, erosion) cause vertical crustal movements.
terms.location: Global - affecting all continental and oceanic crust
terms.example: Scandinavian post-glacial rebound, Himalayan uplift
terms.funFact: Scandinavia is still rising about 1 cm per year as it rebounds from the last ice age - 10,000 years after the ice melted!

terms.name: Isthmus
terms.hook: A narrow land bridge connecting two larger landmasses
terms.definition: A narrow strip of land bordered by water on both sides, connecting two larger land areas. Isthmuses are strategically important for transportation and have been sites of major canal construction.
terms.example: Isthmus of Panama, Isthmus of Suez, Isthmus of Corinth
terms.funFact: The Panama Canal, cutting through the 80-km-wide Isthmus of Panama, saves ships a 15,000-km journey around South America!

terms.name: Jet Stream
terms.hook: Rivers of wind racing through the upper atmosphere
terms.definition: A narrow band of strong westerly winds in the upper troposphere, flowing at speeds of 100-400 km/h. Jet streams influence weather patterns, aircraft routes, and the movement of storm systems.
terms.example: Polar jet stream, subtropical jet stream
terms.funFact: Flying east across the Atlantic is about an hour faster than flying west because planes can ride the jet stream!

terms.name: Jungle
terms.hook: Dense tangles of tropical vegetation nearly impenetrable
terms.definition: Dense, tangled tropical vegetation typically found at the edge of rainforests, along rivers, or in disturbed areas where sunlight reaches the forest floor. Distinct from rainforest interior, which has clearer understory.
terms.formation: Rapid growth in areas of high light availability
terms.example: Amazon jungle, Congo Basin, Southeast Asian jungle
terms.funFact: True jungle only exists where sunlight reaches the ground - in undisturbed rainforest, the understory is surprisingly open!

terms.name: Jupiter
terms.hook: The solar system's giant king with its famous storm
terms.definition: The fifth planet from the Sun and the largest in the solar system, with a mass 2.5 times that of all other planets combined. Jupiter is a gas giant composed mainly of hydrogen and helium, famous for its Great Red Spot.
terms.formation: 4.5 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Great Red Spot, Galilean moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto)
terms.funFact: Jupiter's Great Red Spot is a storm that has been raging for at least 400 years and is bigger than Earth!
terms.etymology: Named after the Roman king of the gods

terms.name: Kame
terms.hook: Hilly mounds of sand and gravel left by glacier meltwater
terms.definition: A steep-sided mound of sand and gravel deposited by meltwater in contact with a glacier. Kames form in depressions on, under, or adjacent to stagnating ice and may contain internal stratification.
terms.formation: Deposition against or within melting ice
terms.example: Scottish kames, New England kames, Midwestern US kames
terms.funFact: Kames often look like giant anthills dotting the landscape - they're prized as sources of sand and gravel!

terms.name: Karst
terms.hook: Landscapes sculpted by dissolving rock into caves and sinkholes
terms.definition: A type of landscape formed by the dissolution of soluble rocks (limestone, dolomite, gypsum), characterized by sinkholes, caves, disappearing streams, and underground drainage systems.
terms.formation: Millions of years of chemical dissolution
terms.example: Karst of Slovenia, Guilin (China), Florida, Yucatan Peninsula
terms.funFact: About 25% of the world's population depends on water from karst aquifers - but these systems are extremely vulnerable to pollution!
terms.etymology: From the Kras/Karst region in Slovenia/Italy

terms.name: Kettle Lake
terms.hook: Lakes filling holes where buried ice finally melted
terms.definition: A depression in glacial drift formed by a buried block of ice melting after a glacier retreats. Kettles often fill with water to become kettle lakes or ponds, common in glaciated landscapes.
terms.formation: Ice block melting after glacial retreat
terms.example: Minnesota 'Land of 10,000 Lakes', Walden Pond, Wisconsin kettles
terms.funFact: Minnesota has 11,842 lakes larger than 10 acres - almost all are kettle lakes from the last ice age!
terms.etymology: From the kettle-like shape of the depression

terms.name: Kuiper Belt
terms.hook: The frozen outer ring where dwarf planets and comets dwell
terms.definition: A circumstellar disc of icy bodies in the outer solar system, extending from Neptune's orbit (~30 AU) to approximately 50 AU from the Sun. The Kuiper Belt contains dwarf planets, comets, and billions of small objects.
terms.formation: Primordial remnants of solar system formation
terms.example: Pluto, Eris, Makemake, Haumea, numerous comets
terms.funFact: The Kuiper Belt contains at least 100,000 objects larger than 100 km - a vast frozen frontier we're only beginning to explore!
terms.etymology: Named after astronomer Gerard Kuiper who predicted its existence

terms.name: La Niña
terms.hook: Pacific cooling that brings opposite extremes from El Niño
terms.definition: A climate pattern characterized by unusually cold ocean temperatures in the equatorial Pacific, the opposite phase of El Niño. La Niña typically brings drought to South America and increased rainfall to Australia.
terms.formation: Strengthening of trade winds and upwelling
terms.example: 2010-2012 La Niña (one of strongest), Australian floods
terms.funFact: La Niña means 'The Girl' in Spanish - it's the sister pattern to El Niño ('The Boy'), often arriving after him!

terms.name: Lacustrine
terms.hook: Everything related to the world of lakes
terms.definition: Relating to or associated with lakes. Lacustrine deposits are sediments laid down in lake environments; lacustrine ecosystems include all organisms and processes within lake habitats.
terms.example: Lacustrine sediments, lacustrine clay, Great Lakes ecosystem
terms.funFact: Some lacustrine sediments contain annual layers (varves) that scientists count like tree rings to date climate events!

terms.name: Lagoon
terms.hook: Calm, shallow waters sheltered from the open sea
terms.definition: A shallow body of water separated from the sea by a barrier beach, coral reef, or similar feature. Lagoons are typically brackish and support unique ecosystems where fresh and salt water mix.
terms.location: Behind barrier islands and inside atolls
terms.formation: Barrier development or coral reef growth
terms.example: Venice Lagoon, Florida lagoons, atoll lagoons (Maldives)
terms.funFact: The lagoon in Kiritimati (Christmas Island) is so shallow and hot that salt crystallizes on its surface!

terms.name: Lake
terms.hook: Standing water collected in nature's basins
terms.definition: A large body of freshwater or saline water surrounded by land. Lakes form in depressions created by glaciers, tectonics, volcanism, or dissolution, and can range from small ponds to vast inland seas.
terms.example: Lake Superior, Caspian Sea, Lake Baikal, Lake Victoria
terms.funFact: Lake Baikal is so deep (1,642m) and ancient (25 million years) that it contains more water than all the Great Lakes combined!

terms.name: Land Breeze
terms.hook: Cool night winds flowing from land toward warmer sea
terms.definition: A local wind blowing from land to sea at night, caused by the land cooling faster than the ocean. The cooler, denser air over land flows toward the relatively warmer sea surface.
terms.formation: Nightly - differential cooling of land and sea
terms.example: Nighttime coastal breezes, offshore winds
terms.funFact: Fishermen traditionally sailed out to sea on the land breeze at night and returned on the sea breeze during the day!

terms.name: Landform
terms.hook: Any natural feature that shapes Earth's surface
terms.definition: A natural feature of Earth's solid surface, such as a mountain, valley, or plain. Landforms result from tectonic, volcanic, erosional, or depositional processes and range from microscale to continental.
terms.example: Mountains, valleys, plateaus, dunes, deltas
terms.funFact: Geomorphologists have identified and named over 100 distinct types of landforms - and new ones are still being discovered on other planets!

terms.name: Landslide
terms.hook: When gravity wins and hillsides come crashing down
terms.definition: The movement of rock, soil, and debris down a slope under the influence of gravity. Landslides can be triggered by earthquakes, rainfall, volcanic activity, erosion, or human activities.
terms.formation: Sudden to gradual depending on type
terms.example: 2014 Oso landslide (Washington), Vajont Dam disaster (Italy)
terms.funFact: The 1958 Lituya Bay megatsunami was triggered by a landslide that created a wave 524 meters high - taller than any skyscraper!

terms.name: Latitude
terms.hook: Measuring how far north or south on Earth's globe
terms.definition: The angular distance north or south of the Earth's equator, measured in degrees from 0° at the equator to 90° at the poles. Lines of latitude (parallels) run east-west around the globe.
terms.example: Equator (0°), Tropic of Cancer (23.5°N), Arctic Circle (66.5°N)
terms.funFact: Latitude determines how long days are - at the Arctic Circle, the sun never sets on summer solstice!

terms.name: Laurasia
terms.hook: The ancient northern supercontinent before the Atlantic existed
terms.definition: The northern portion of the supercontinent Pangaea, comprising present-day North America, Europe, and Asia (excluding India). Laurasia began separating from Gondwana about 200 million years ago.
terms.example: Former single landmass including North America, Europe, Asia
terms.funFact: When Laurasia existed, you could have walked from New York to Paris - the Atlantic Ocean didn't exist yet!

terms.name: Lava
terms.hook: Molten rock breaking free to reshape the surface
terms.definition: Magma that has erupted onto Earth's surface through a volcanic vent or fissure. Lava flows can spread for kilometers, and its composition determines whether it flows smoothly (basaltic) or moves slowly (rhyolitic).
terms.formation: Volcanic eruption bringing magma to surface
terms.example: Hawaiian lava flows, Icelandic eruptions, Kilauea
terms.funFact: Lava can reach temperatures of 1,200°C - hot enough to glow bright orange and melt steel!
terms.etymology: From Italian 'lava', from Latin 'labes' (a fall, slide)

terms.name: Lava Tube
terms.hook: Underground tunnels carved by rivers of molten rock
terms.definition: A natural conduit formed when the surface of a flowing lava stream cools and solidifies while molten lava continues flowing underneath. When the lava drains, it leaves a tube-shaped cave.
terms.example: Kazumura Cave (Hawaii), Thurston Lava Tube, Cheju Island tubes (Korea)
terms.funFact: Kazumura Cave in Hawaii is 65.5 km long - the world's longest lava tube, and it descends over 1,100 meters!

terms.name: Leaching
terms.hook: Water dissolving and carrying soil nutrients downward
terms.definition: The removal of soluble materials from soil by percolating water. Leaching can deplete soil nutrients, move pollutants to groundwater, or concentrate minerals in lower soil horizons.
terms.formation: Continuous with water movement through soil
terms.example: Nutrient leaching in tropical soils, iron leaching in podzols
terms.funFact: Heavy leaching in tropical rainforests makes their soils surprisingly infertile - most nutrients are locked in the living vegetation!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'leccan' meaning 'to water, moisten'

terms.name: Leeward
terms.hook: The sheltered side where wind's shadow falls
terms.definition: The side of a landmass sheltered from the prevailing wind, opposite to windward. Leeward slopes typically receive less precipitation, creating rain shadows and drier conditions.
terms.location: Relative to landforms and wind direction
terms.formation: Determined by wind patterns and topography
terms.example: Leeward side of Hawaii, eastern Sierra Nevada, Canterbury Plains (NZ)
terms.funFact: The leeward side of Hawaii receives only 400mm of rain per year, while the windward side gets over 10,000mm!

terms.name: Levee
terms.hook: Natural or built walls keeping rivers in their banks
terms.definition: A raised bank along a river, either naturally formed by sediment deposition during floods or artificially constructed to prevent flooding. Natural levees are highest near the channel and slope away.
terms.example: Mississippi River levees, Sacramento River levees, Netherlands dikes
terms.funFact: The US has over 160,000 km of levees - enough to circle Earth four times - but many are in poor condition!

terms.name: Light-Year
terms.hook: The distance light travels in one year of racing through space
terms.definition: A unit of astronomical distance equal to the distance light travels in one year: approximately 9.46 trillion kilometers (5.88 trillion miles). Light-years measure the vast distances between stars and galaxies.
terms.formation: Defined unit based on speed of light
terms.example: Proxima Centauri (4.24 ly), Milky Way diameter (~100,000 ly), Andromeda (2.5 million ly)
terms.funFact: When you look at the Andromeda Galaxy, you're seeing light that left 2.5 million years ago - before humans existed!

terms.name: Limestone
terms.hook: Ancient sea creatures compressed into stone
terms.definition: A sedimentary rock composed primarily of calcium carbonate (CaCite), often formed from the accumulated remains of marine organisms like coral, foraminifera, and shellfish. Limestone dissolves in weak acid, forming karst landscapes.
terms.formation: Millions of years of marine deposition
terms.example: White Cliffs of Dover, Florida limestone, karst regions
terms.funFact: The Great Pyramid of Giza contains 2.3 million limestone blocks - ancient Egyptians were building with fossilized sea creatures!

terms.name: Lithosphere
terms.hook: Earth's rigid outer shell riding on plastic rock below
terms.definition: The rigid outer layer of Earth, comprising the crust and uppermost solid mantle. The lithosphere is broken into tectonic plates that float on the underlying asthenosphere and drive plate tectonics.
terms.example: Oceanic lithosphere (thinner), continental lithosphere (thicker, older)
terms.funFact: Oceanic lithosphere is constantly being created at mid-ocean ridges and destroyed at subduction zones - it's never older than 200 million years!

terms.name: Loam
terms.hook: The perfect balance of sand, silt, and clay for growing
terms.definition: A fertile soil type with a balanced mixture of sand, silt, and clay, typically 40% sand, 40% silt, and 20% clay. Loam retains moisture while draining well, making it ideal for agriculture.
terms.formation: Weathering and mixing of parent materials
terms.example: Prairie loams, alluvial loams, garden soil
terms.funFact: Gardeners call loam 'black gold' because it's the ideal growing medium - it took nature thousands of years to create!

terms.name: Loess
terms.hook: Wind-blown silt blanketing landscapes in fertile gold
terms.definition: A deposit of wind-blown silt, typically yellowish-brown and highly fertile. Loess forms thick deposits near glacial outwash plains and deserts, where wind picks up fine sediments and deposits them downwind.
terms.location: Mid-latitudes, near former glaciers and deserts
terms.formation: Thousands of years of wind deposition
terms.example: Chinese Loess Plateau, Mississippi Valley, European loess belt
terms.funFact: The Loess Plateau in China has deposits over 300 meters thick - the entire landscape is built from dust blown from Central Asian deserts!

terms.name: Longitude
terms.hook: Measuring how far east or west around Earth's globe
terms.definition: The angular distance east or west of the Prime Meridian, measured in degrees from 0° to 180° E or W. Lines of longitude (meridians) run from pole to pole and converge at the poles.
terms.example: Prime Meridian (0°), International Date Line (~180°), New York (~74°W)
terms.funFact: Sailors couldn't accurately determine longitude until 1761, when John Harrison invented a precise marine chronometer!

terms.name: Longshore Drift
terms.hook: Waves pushing sand along the shore in endless procession
terms.definition: The movement of sediment along a coast parallel to the shoreline, caused by waves approaching the beach at an angle. Longshore drift shapes beaches, builds spits, and can starve downdrift beaches of sand.
terms.location: All beaches with oblique wave approach
terms.example: Santa Barbara beach erosion, spit formation, coastal engineering problems
terms.funFact: Longshore drift can move millions of tons of sand per year along a single beach - it never stops working!

terms.name: Lunar Eclipse
terms.hook: Earth's shadow painting the Moon blood red
terms.definition: An eclipse occurring when Earth passes between the Sun and Moon, casting its shadow on the Moon. During a total lunar eclipse, the Moon appears red due to sunlight filtered through Earth's atmosphere.
terms.example: Blood moons, total lunar eclipses
terms.funFact: Lunar eclipses turn the Moon red because Earth's atmosphere scatters blue light away - you're seeing every sunset on Earth projected onto the Moon!

terms.name: Maar
terms.hook: Explosive craters born when magma meets groundwater
terms.definition: A low-relief volcanic crater formed by phreatomagmatic (steam) explosions when magma contacts groundwater. Maars are typically surrounded by a low ring of ejected debris and often filled with lakes.
terms.example: Eifel maars (Germany), Ukinrek Maars (Alaska), Maar lakes
terms.funFact: The Eifel region in Germany has so many maar lakes that Romans called it 'the land of volcanic lakes'!

terms.name: Magma
terms.hook: Molten rock stirring in Earth's fiery depths
terms.definition: Molten rock beneath Earth's surface, containing dissolved gases and suspended crystals. When magma reaches the surface through volcanic eruptions, it becomes lava. Magma composition determines eruption style.
terms.formation: Partial melting of rock at depth
terms.example: Basaltic magma, andesitic magma, rhyolitic magma
terms.funFact: Magma chambers can be as large as mountain ranges - the one beneath Yellowstone is over 70 km long!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'magma' meaning 'thick unguent, kneaded mass'

terms.name: Earth's Magnetic Field
terms.hook: Our invisible shield deflecting deadly solar radiation
terms.definition: The magnetic field generated by Earth's liquid outer core, extending into space as the magnetosphere. The magnetic field protects Earth from solar radiation, enables navigation, and has reversed polarity many times.
terms.formation: Generated by convection in liquid outer core
terms.example: Magnetic poles, compass navigation, aurora formation
terms.funFact: Earth's magnetic poles have reversed about 183 times in the past 83 million years - and we're overdue for another flip!

terms.name: Magnetosphere
terms.hook: Earth's magnetic bubble protecting us from space weather
terms.definition: The region of space surrounding Earth dominated by Earth's magnetic field. The magnetosphere deflects most solar wind particles, protects the atmosphere from erosion, and traps charged particles in radiation belts.
terms.formation: Interaction of magnetic field with solar wind
terms.example: Van Allen radiation belts, magnetotail, aurora formation zone
terms.funFact: Without the magnetosphere, solar wind would strip away Earth's atmosphere within a billion years - like what happened to Mars!

terms.name: Mangrove
terms.hook: Salt-tolerant trees walking on water at land's edge
terms.definition: Salt-tolerant trees and shrubs growing in tropical and subtropical intertidal zones. Mangroves have specialized adaptations like aerial roots and salt excretion, forming critical coastal ecosystems.
terms.example: Sundarbans (Bangladesh/India), Florida mangroves, Queensland mangroves
terms.funFact: Mangroves store up to 10 times more carbon per hectare than terrestrial forests - they're climate change superheroes!

terms.name: Mantle
terms.hook: The thick rocky layer between Earth's crust and core
terms.definition: The layer of Earth between the crust and core, about 2,900 km thick and comprising 84% of Earth's volume. The mantle is solid but flows slowly over geological time, driving plate tectonics through convection.
terms.location: Between crust (~35 km) and core (~2,900 km)
terms.example: Upper mantle, lower mantle, mantle plumes
terms.funFact: Mantle rock rises in plumes so slowly that the journey from core-mantle boundary to surface takes 200 million years!

terms.name: Map
terms.hook: Earth shrunk to fit in your hands or on your screen
terms.definition: A symbolic representation of selected characteristics of a place, usually drawn on a flat surface. Maps show spatial relationships and can represent physical features, political boundaries, or thematic data.
terms.location: Represents any part of Earth or space
terms.formation: Created through surveying and cartographic techniques
terms.example: Topographic maps, political maps, road maps, thematic maps
terms.funFact: The oldest known map is a clay tablet from Babylon dating to 2300 BCE - humans have been mapping for over 4,000 years!

terms.name: Marble
terms.hook: Limestone transformed by heat into crystalline beauty
terms.definition: A metamorphic rock formed when limestone or dolomite is subjected to heat and pressure. Marble consists of recrystallized carbonate minerals and is prized for sculpture and architecture due to its beauty and workability.
terms.formation: Regional or contact metamorphism of carbite rocks
terms.example: Carrara marble (Italy), Taj Mahal, Greek sculptures
terms.funFact: Michelangelo's David was carved from a single block of Carrara marble that weighed over 6 tons!

terms.name: Mare (Lunar)
terms.hook: Dark ancient lava plains mistaken for seas on the Moon
terms.definition: A large, dark, basaltic plain on the Moon, formed by ancient volcanic eruptions. Early astronomers thought these dark areas were seas, hence the Latin name 'mare' (sea). Maria cover about 16% of the lunar surface.
terms.formation: 3-4 billion years ago from basaltic lava flows
terms.example: Mare Tranquillitatis (Sea of Tranquility), Mare Imbrium, Mare Serenitatis
terms.funFact: The Apollo 11 astronauts landed in Mare Tranquillitatis - the 'Sea of Tranquility' was actually bone-dry ancient lava!

terms.name: Mariana Trench
terms.hook: Earth's deepest scar plunging 11 kilometers into darkness
terms.definition: The deepest oceanic trench on Earth, located in the western Pacific Ocean. The Challenger Deep within the trench reaches 10,994 meters below sea level - deeper than Mount Everest is tall.
terms.location: Western Pacific Ocean, east of the Mariana Islands
terms.formation: Pacific Plate subducting beneath Philippine Plate
terms.example: Challenger Deep (deepest point), Sirena Deep
terms.funFact: More people have walked on the Moon (12) than have visited the bottom of the Mariana Trench (3 crewed missions)!
terms.etymology: Named after the nearby Mariana Islands

terms.name: Marine
terms.hook: Everything belonging to the salty realm of the sea
terms.definition: Relating to or found in the sea. Marine environments include all oceanic habitats from coastal waters to the deep sea, and marine life encompasses organisms adapted to saltwater conditions.
terms.example: Marine ecosystems, marine biology, marine geology
terms.funFact: Marine phytoplankton produce 50-80% of Earth's oxygen - the ocean is our planet's real lung!

terms.name: Maritime Climate
terms.hook: Ocean's moderating breath keeping temperatures mild
terms.definition: A climate influenced by proximity to the ocean, characterized by mild winters, cool summers, and relatively small temperature ranges. Maritime climates typically have high humidity and frequent precipitation.
terms.example: British Isles, Pacific Northwest, New Zealand
terms.funFact: London and Moscow are at similar latitudes, but London's maritime climate keeps it 15°C warmer in winter!

terms.name: Mars
terms.hook: The rusty red world where robots roam and humans dream
terms.definition: The fourth planet from the Sun, known as the 'Red Planet' due to iron oxide on its surface. Mars has the solar system's largest volcano (Olympus Mons) and canyon (Valles Marineris), and shows evidence of ancient water.
terms.formation: 4.6 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Olympus Mons (tallest mountain), Valles Marineris (largest canyon), polar ice caps
terms.funFact: A day on Mars (called a 'sol') is only 37 minutes longer than an Earth day - almost perfect for human adaptation!
terms.etymology: Named after the Roman god of war

terms.name: Marsh
terms.hook: Waterlogged lands where grasses and reeds rule
terms.definition: A type of wetland characterized by herbaceous plants (grasses, reeds, sedges) rather than woody plants. Marshes can be freshwater or saltwater and provide critical habitat for wildlife and water filtration.
terms.example: Everglades (Florida), salt marshes, prairie potholes
terms.funFact: Salt marshes are among the most productive ecosystems on Earth - they produce more organic matter per acre than most farmland!

terms.name: Mass Extinction
terms.hook: When Earth erases most species and starts fresh
terms.definition: A widespread and rapid decrease in biodiversity on Earth, typically when more than 75% of species disappear in a geologically short time. Earth has experienced five major mass extinctions, with some scientists arguing we're in a sixth.
terms.formation: Various causes - asteroids, volcanism, climate change
terms.example: End-Permian (96% species), K-Pg/dinosaur extinction, current biodiversity crisis
terms.funFact: The End-Permian extinction 252 million years ago was so severe it's called 'The Great Dying' - it took life 10 million years to recover!

terms.name: Mass Wasting
terms.hook: Gravity pulling everything downhill in slow or sudden motion
terms.definition: The bulk movement of soil, rock, and debris down a slope under the direct influence of gravity. Mass wasting includes landslides, rockfalls, mudflows, and slow creep, and is a major landscape-shaping process.
terms.example: Landslides, rockfalls, debris flows, soil creep
terms.funFact: Soil creep is so slow you can't see it, but it's why old gravestones and fence posts lean downhill!

terms.name: Meander
terms.hook: Rivers lazily looping across their floodplains
terms.definition: A sinuous curve or loop in a river channel, formed as the river erodes its outer bank and deposits sediment on the inner bank. Meanders migrate across floodplains and can eventually be cut off to form oxbow lakes.
terms.example: Mississippi River meanders, Amazon meanders, Thames
terms.funFact: The word 'meander' comes from the Menderes River in Turkey, famous for its winding path since ancient times!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'Maiandros', the Menderes River in Turkey

terms.name: Mediterranean Climate
terms.hook: Dry summers and mild, wet winters by the wine-growing seas
terms.definition: A climate characterized by hot, dry summers and mild, wet winters. Found on the western sides of continents between 30-45° latitude, this climate supports distinctive vegetation like chaparral and is ideal for viticulture.
terms.location: Five regions globally (Mediterranean, California, Chile, South Africa, Australia)
terms.formation: Subtropical high pressure in summer, westerlies in winter
terms.example: Mediterranean Basin, California, Central Chile, Cape Town region
terms.funFact: Mediterranean climates exist on only 2% of Earth's land but produce most of the world's wine and olive oil!
terms.etymology: Named after the Mediterranean Sea region

terms.name: Megalopolis
terms.hook: When cities grow so large they merge into super-regions
terms.definition: An extensive metropolitan area or chain of continuous urban areas that have merged together. Megalopolises contain tens of millions of people and represent extreme urbanization.
terms.formation: Decades of urban sprawl and growth
terms.example: BosWash (Boston-Washington), Pearl River Delta, Tokyo-Yokohama
terms.funFact: The Pearl River Delta megalopolis in China has over 65 million people - larger than the population of France!

terms.name: Meltwater
terms.hook: Glaciers bleeding rivers of ice-cold water
terms.definition: Water released by melting of snow or ice, including glacial ice, snowpack, and permafrost. Meltwater creates distinctive landforms, supplies rivers, and is a critical water resource in many regions.
terms.example: Glacial meltwater streams, spring snowmelt, permafrost thaw
terms.funFact: Glacial meltwater is so sediment-rich it appears milky white - early settlers called it 'glacial milk'!

terms.name: Mercator Projection
terms.hook: The map that made Greenland look bigger than Africa
terms.definition: A cylindrical map projection developed by Gerardus Mercator in 1569, preserving angles and shapes but distorting size, especially near the poles. Mercator maps were revolutionary for navigation but misrepresent relative areas.
terms.location: Used for world maps and navigation
terms.formation: Mathematical transformation of sphere to cylinder
terms.example: Navigation charts, Google Maps (modified), world maps
terms.funFact: On a Mercator map, Greenland appears larger than Africa - but Africa is actually 14 times bigger!
terms.etymology: Named after Flemish cartographer Gerardus Mercator

terms.name: Mercury
terms.hook: The sun-scorched speedster racing around our star
terms.definition: The smallest and innermost planet in the solar system, orbiting the Sun in just 88 Earth days. Mercury has extreme temperature variations (from -180°C to 430°C) and a heavily cratered surface resembling the Moon.
terms.formation: 4.5 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Caloris Basin (large impact crater), scarps, wrinkle ridges
terms.funFact: A day on Mercury (sunrise to sunrise) takes 176 Earth days - longer than its 88-day year!
terms.etymology: Named after the Roman messenger god

terms.name: Meridian
terms.hook: Imaginary lines running from pole to pole
terms.definition: A line of longitude running from the North Pole to the South Pole, connecting all points of equal longitude. The Prime Meridian (0°) passes through Greenwich, England, and serves as the reference for all other meridians.
terms.example: Prime Meridian (0°), International Date Line (~180°)
terms.funFact: Before the Prime Meridian was standardized in 1884, there were over 20 different 'prime meridians' used by different countries!

terms.name: Mesa
terms.hook: Flat-topped tables of rock standing above the desert
terms.definition: An isolated flat-topped hill with steep sides, larger than a butte but smaller than a plateau. Mesas form when erosion removes surrounding softer rock, leaving a resistant cap rock protecting the underlying layers.
terms.formation: Millions of years of differential erosion
terms.example: Grand Mesa (Colorado), Monument Valley mesas, Spanish mesas
terms.funFact: Grand Mesa in Colorado is the world's largest flat-topped mountain - over 1,300 square kilometers of elevated tableland!

terms.name: Mesosphere
terms.hook: The coldest layer where meteors burn bright
terms.definition: The layer of Earth's atmosphere above the stratosphere, extending from about 50 to 85 km altitude. The mesosphere is characterized by decreasing temperature with altitude and is where most meteors burn up.
terms.example: Meteor trails, noctilucent clouds
terms.funFact: The mesosphere is the coldest place in the Earth system, reaching -90°C - colder than Antarctica or anywhere on Earth's surface!

terms.name: Mesozoic Era
terms.hook: The Age of Dinosaurs spanning 186 million years
terms.definition: A geological era lasting from 252 to 66 million years ago, comprising the Triassic, Jurassic, and Cretaceous periods. The Mesozoic saw the rise and dominance of dinosaurs, the first mammals and birds, and flowering plants.
terms.example: Dinosaur fossils, early mammals, Pangaea breakup
terms.funFact: If the Mesozoic Era were compressed into one 24-hour day, humans would appear in the last 0.1 seconds!

terms.name: Metamorphic Rock
terms.hook: Rocks reborn through heat and pressure's transformation
terms.definition: Rock that has been changed from its original form by heat, pressure, or chemical processes without melting. Metamorphism creates new minerals and textures, transforming rocks like limestone into marble or shale into slate.
terms.location: Mountain belts, contact zones, deep crust
terms.example: Marble, slate, quartzite, gneiss, schist
terms.funFact: The beautiful swirled patterns in metamorphic rocks formed while the rock was being squeezed like toothpaste at temperatures exceeding 300°C!

terms.name: Meteor
terms.hook: Shooting stars streaking bright across the night
terms.definition: The visible streak of light produced when a meteoroid enters Earth's atmosphere and burns up due to friction. Meteors are commonly called 'shooting stars' and occur when particles as small as sand grains vaporize.
terms.example: Perseid meteor shower, Leonids, sporadic meteors
terms.funFact: About 48 tons of meteoric material falls on Earth every day - mostly as microscopic dust particles!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'meteōros' meaning 'high in the air'

terms.name: Meteorite
terms.hook: Space rocks that survived the fiery fall to Earth
terms.definition: A solid piece of debris from a meteoroid or asteroid that survives passage through the atmosphere and lands on Earth's surface. Meteorites are classified as stony, iron, or stony-iron based on composition.
terms.example: Hoba meteorite (Namibia), Allende meteorite, Murchison meteorite
terms.funFact: The Hoba meteorite in Namibia weighs 60 tons and has never been moved from where it fell - it's the largest known meteorite on Earth!

terms.name: Meteorology
terms.hook: The science of understanding and predicting weather
terms.definition: The scientific study of the atmosphere and its phenomena, especially weather and weather forecasting. Meteorology examines temperature, precipitation, atmospheric pressure, wind, and climate patterns.
terms.formation: Ancient origins, modern science from 17th century
terms.example: Weather forecasting, climate modeling, severe storm research
terms.funFact: Modern weather forecasting is 90% accurate for 5-day forecasts - but only about 50% accurate at 10 days due to chaos theory!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'meteorologia' - study of atmospheric phenomena

terms.name: Microclimate
terms.hook: Tiny climate zones hiding in gardens and valleys
terms.definition: The climate of a small, specific area that differs from the surrounding region due to local factors like topography, vegetation, or urban development. Microclimates can exist at scales from centimeters to kilometers.
terms.location: Local areas within larger climate zones
terms.example: Urban heat islands, south-facing slopes, forest clearings
terms.funFact: A south-facing slope in the Northern Hemisphere can be warm enough to grow grapes where the surrounding area cannot support them!

terms.name: Mid-Atlantic Ridge
terms.hook: Earth's longest mountain range hidden beneath the waves
terms.definition: A submarine mountain range extending the length of the Atlantic Ocean, marking the divergent boundary between tectonic plates. The ridge is where new ocean floor is created as plates spread apart at 2.5 cm per year.
terms.example: Iceland (above-water portion), Azores, Tristan da Cunha
terms.funFact: Iceland is the only place where the Mid-Atlantic Ridge rises above sea level - you can literally see two continents pulling apart!

terms.name: Mid-Ocean Ridge
terms.hook: Where new Earth is born along a 65,000-kilometer seam
terms.definition: A continuous underwater mountain system formed by plate tectonics where oceanic plates diverge. Mid-ocean ridges encircle the globe for 65,000 km and are sites of volcanic activity and seafloor spreading.
terms.formation: Continuous volcanic activity at divergent boundaries
terms.example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East Pacific Rise, Indian Ocean Ridge
terms.funFact: The mid-ocean ridge system is the longest mountain range on Earth - more than 10 times longer than the Andes!

terms.name: Migration
terms.hook: Epic journeys animals make to follow seasons and food
terms.definition: The seasonal movement of animals from one region to another for breeding, feeding, or climate reasons. Migration can cover thousands of kilometers and involves remarkable navigation abilities.
terms.example: Arctic tern migration (71,000 km), wildebeest, monarch butterflies, salmon
terms.funFact: The Arctic tern migrates from Arctic to Antarctic and back each year - the longest migration of any animal, seeing two summers annually!
terms.etymology: From Latin 'migrare' meaning 'to move from one place to another'

terms.name: Milky Way
terms.hook: Our cosmic home - a spiral of 400 billion stars
terms.definition: The barred spiral galaxy containing our solar system, visible as a hazy band of light stretching across the night sky. The Milky Way contains 100-400 billion stars and is about 100,000 light-years in diameter.
terms.example: Galactic center, spiral arms, Local Group member
terms.funFact: You can only see about 2,500 stars with the naked eye, but the Milky Way contains at least 100 billion - and possibly 400 billion!
terms.etymology: From Latin 'via lactea' (milky way) - from its milky appearance

terms.name: Mineral
terms.hook: Nature's building blocks with fixed chemical recipes
terms.definition: A naturally occurring, inorganic solid with a definite chemical composition and crystalline structure. Over 5,000 minerals have been identified, forming the basic components of rocks and geological processes.
terms.example: Quartz, feldspar, calcite, diamond, gold
terms.funFact: The most abundant mineral in Earth's crust is feldspar (about 60%) - you're probably standing on it right now!

terms.name: Mirage
terms.hook: Heat bending light into shimmering illusions
terms.definition: An optical phenomenon where light rays are bent by temperature gradients in air, creating displaced or distorted images. Mirages often appear as water on hot roads or inverted images in deserts.
terms.example: Desert mirages, highway 'water', Fata Morgana
terms.funFact: Fata Morgana mirages can make ships appear to float above the water or stretch buildings into impossible towers!
terms.etymology: From French, from Arabic 'sarāb' via Latin

terms.name: Mistral
terms.hook: The fierce cold wind that funnels down the Rhône Valley
terms.definition: A strong, cold, dry northwesterly wind that blows from central France toward the Mediterranean coast, particularly through the Rhône Valley. The Mistral can exceed 100 km/h and blow for days.
terms.formation: High pressure over central Europe, low pressure over Mediterranean
terms.example: Provence, Rhône Valley, French Riviera
terms.funFact: Van Gogh mentioned the Mistral in his letters, saying it drove him half-mad while painting in Provence!

terms.name: Mohorovičić Discontinuity
terms.hook: The invisible boundary where crust gives way to mantle
terms.definition: The boundary between Earth's crust and mantle, marked by a sharp increase in seismic wave velocity. The 'Moho' lies at about 5-10 km depth under oceans and 30-70 km under continents.
