terms.name: Sea Breeze
terms.hook: Cool ocean winds blowing inland on warm afternoons
terms.definition: A local wind blowing from sea to land during the day, caused by the differential heating of land and water. Land heats faster than water, causing air to rise over land and cooler sea air to flow in.
terms.formation: Daily - differential heating of land and sea
terms.example: Afternoon coastal breezes, 'doctor' winds
terms.funFact: Sea breezes can penetrate 40-50 km inland and can lower coastal temperatures by 5-10°C on hot days!

terms.name: Seafloor Spreading
terms.hook: New ocean floor born at underwater mountain ridges
terms.definition: The process by which new oceanic crust is formed at mid-ocean ridges as tectonic plates diverge and magma rises to fill the gap. Seafloor spreading is the mechanism driving continental drift.
terms.example: Mid-Atlantic Ridge, East Pacific Rise
terms.funFact: The Atlantic Ocean is widening by about 2.5 cm per year - the Americas are slowly moving away from Europe and Africa!

terms.name: Sea Ice
terms.hook: Frozen ocean water forming and melting with the seasons
terms.definition: Ice formed by the freezing of seawater, as opposed to icebergs which come from glaciers. Sea ice forms a frozen crust over polar oceans and varies dramatically with seasons, shrinking in summer.
terms.example: Arctic sea ice, Antarctic sea ice, pack ice
terms.funFact: Arctic sea ice has declined by about 13% per decade since 1979 - the Arctic could be ice-free in summer within decades!

terms.name: Sea Level
terms.hook: The baseline from which we measure Earth's heights and depths
terms.definition: The average height of the sea surface, used as a datum for measuring elevation. Mean sea level varies locally due to tides, currents, and atmospheric pressure, and is rising globally due to climate change.
terms.example: Mean sea level, high tide, low tide
terms.funFact: Global sea level is rising about 3.3 mm per year - and accelerating. It's risen 20 cm since 1900!

terms.name: Sea Stack
terms.hook: Isolated rock pillars standing defiant against the waves
terms.definition: A vertical column of rock rising from the sea near a coast, formed by wave erosion of a headland. Sea stacks are remnants of former coastline, left standing after softer surrounding rock eroded away.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of wave erosion
terms.example: Twelve Apostles (Australia), Old Man of Hoy (Scotland), Haystack Rock (Oregon)
terms.funFact: The 'Twelve Apostles' in Australia originally had different names and there were never twelve - now only eight remain!

terms.name: Seamount
terms.hook: Underwater mountains that never reach the surface
terms.definition: An underwater mountain rising from the ocean floor but not reaching the surface. Seamounts are typically volcanic in origin, often forming over hotspots, and provide important habitat for deep-sea life.
terms.formation: Volcanic activity on the ocean floor
terms.example: Emperor Seamounts, New England Seamounts, Davidson Seamount
terms.funFact: There are estimated to be over 100,000 seamounts in the world's oceans - more than all the mountains on land!

terms.name: Season
terms.hook: Earth's tilt creating annual cycles of change
terms.definition: A division of the year based on changes in weather, ecology, and daylight hours. Seasons result from Earth's axial tilt causing different parts of the planet to receive more direct sunlight at different times.
terms.formation: Earth's 23.5° axial tilt relative to orbital plane
terms.example: Spring, summer, autumn/fall, winter (temperate); wet/dry (tropical)
terms.funFact: When it's summer in the Northern Hemisphere, Earth is actually farthest from the Sun - seasons are caused by tilt, not distance!
terms.etymology: From Old French 'seison', from Latin 'satio' (sowing time)

terms.name: Sediment
terms.hook: Loose particles waiting to become solid rock
terms.definition: Naturally occurring material broken down by weathering and erosion and transported by wind, water, or ice. Sediment ranges from clay particles to boulders and eventually lithifies into sedimentary rock.
terms.example: Sand, mud, gravel, silt, clay
terms.funFact: Rivers carry about 20 billion tons of sediment to the oceans each year - that's 3 tons for every person on Earth!

terms.name: Sedimentary Rock
terms.hook: Layers of Earth history compressed into stone
terms.definition: Rock formed from the accumulation and cementation of sediments deposited by water, wind, or ice. Sedimentary rocks cover 75% of Earth's land surface and contain fossils that record life's history.
terms.formation: Deposition, compaction, and cementation over millions of years
terms.example: Sandstone, limestone, shale, conglomerate
terms.funFact: Sedimentary rocks are like books - each layer is a page recording conditions at the time it was deposited!

terms.name: Seismic
terms.hook: The shaking energy released by earthquakes
terms.definition: Relating to earthquakes or Earth vibrations. Seismic waves are energy waves generated by earthquakes that travel through Earth, providing information about both the earthquake and Earth's interior structure.
terms.example: Seismic waves, seismic hazard, seismic survey
terms.funFact: Seismic waves from large earthquakes can travel completely through Earth and be detected on the opposite side of the planet!

terms.name: Seismograph
terms.hook: Instruments that record Earth's every tremor
terms.definition: An instrument that detects and records seismic waves caused by earthquakes, explosions, or other ground vibrations. Modern seismographs can detect ground motion as small as a billionth of a meter.
terms.example: Broadband seismograph, strong-motion sensor, seismic array
terms.funFact: The first seismograph was invented in China in 132 AD - a bronze vessel with dragons that would drop balls to indicate earthquake direction!

terms.name: Shale
terms.hook: Fine-grained rock splitting into thin sheets
terms.definition: A fine-grained sedimentary rock formed from compacted clay and silt. Shale is the most common sedimentary rock and can split into thin layers along bedding planes. It's the source rock for most petroleum.
terms.formation: Compaction of clay and mud over millions of years
terms.example: Marcellus Shale, oil shale, black shale (organic-rich)
terms.funFact: The shale gas revolution in the US was only possible because of hydraulic fracturing - shale is so impermeable that gas can't flow out naturally!

terms.name: Shield (Continental)
terms.hook: Ancient stable hearts of continents
terms.definition: A large area of exposed Precambrian crystalline rock that forms the stable core of a continental mass. Shields have been tectonically stable for hundreds of millions of years and contain some of Earth's oldest rocks.
terms.formation: Billions of years of geological processes
terms.example: Canadian Shield, Baltic Shield, Australian Shield, Brazilian Shield
terms.funFact: The Canadian Shield contains rocks over 4 billion years old - almost as old as Earth itself!
terms.etymology: From the shield-like appearance on geological maps

terms.name: Sill
terms.hook: Sheets of magma squeezed between rock layers
terms.definition: A tabular sheet of igneous rock that has been intruded between older layers of sedimentary rock, bedding planes, or other sheets. Sills are concordant, meaning they follow existing rock structures.
terms.example: Palisades Sill (New Jersey), Great Whin Sill (England)
terms.funFact: The Palisades along the Hudson River are a famous sill - the dark cliffs you see from New York City are ancient magma!

terms.name: Silt
terms.hook: Fine sediment between sand and clay in size
terms.definition: Sedimentary particles finer than sand but coarser than clay, typically 0.004-0.062 mm in diameter. Silt is transported by water and wind and deposited in river floodplains, lakes, and as loess.
terms.example: River silt, loess deposits, reservoir sediment
terms.funFact: The Nile's annual floods deposited fertile silt that made ancient Egypt possible - the word 'chemistry' comes from 'kemet' (black land, the silt)!
terms.etymology: From Middle English, probably from Scandinavian origin

terms.name: Sinkhole
terms.hook: When the ground suddenly drops into hidden cavities
terms.definition: A depression or hole in the ground caused by collapse of the surface layer into underground cavities, typically in limestone regions. Sinkholes can form gradually or catastrophically.
terms.formation: Dissolution and collapse of soluble rock
terms.example: Florida sinkholes, Yucatan cenotes, Chinese karst
terms.funFact: A sinkhole in Guatemala City in 2010 was 60 meters deep and 20 meters wide - it swallowed an entire building!

terms.name: Sleet
terms.hook: Ice pellets bouncing down from freezing skies
terms.definition: Precipitation consisting of small ice pellets formed when raindrops freeze as they fall through a subfreezing layer of air. Sleet differs from freezing rain, which freezes on contact with cold surfaces.
terms.location: Cold regions during certain weather conditions
terms.formation: Rain freezing in cold air layer near surface
terms.example: Winter storms, ice storms
terms.funFact: In British English, 'sleet' means a mixture of rain and snow - the American meaning (ice pellets) is called 'ice pellets' in Britain!
terms.etymology: From Middle English 'slete', of Germanic origin

terms.name: Slope
terms.hook: Inclined surfaces connecting highs and lows
terms.definition: An inclined surface of the ground, characterized by gradient (steepness), aspect (direction faced), and shape (convex, concave, straight). Slopes are fundamental landforms where many geomorphic processes occur.
terms.example: Hillslopes, mountain slopes, coastal cliffs
terms.funFact: The angle of repose - the steepest stable slope for loose material - is about 34° for dry sand. Any steeper and it avalanches!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'āslūpan' meaning 'to slip away'

terms.name: Slump
terms.hook: When hillsides suddenly rotate and slide downward
terms.definition: A type of mass wasting where material moves downslope along a curved surface, causing backward rotation. Slumps leave characteristic crescent-shaped scarps at the top and bulging toes at the base.
terms.example: Coastal cliff slumps, road cut failures, reservoir bank slumps
terms.funFact: Slumps often leave tilted trees on the slump block - they lean uphill rather than downhill, a telltale sign of this type of landslide!
terms.etymology: From Middle Low German 'slumpen' meaning 'to fall'

terms.name: Smog
terms.hook: Toxic haze from pollution cloaking cities
terms.definition: Air pollution consisting of fog combined with smoke and other atmospheric pollutants. Photochemical smog forms when sunlight reacts with nitrogen oxides and hydrocarbons, producing ozone and other harmful compounds.
terms.example: Los Angeles smog, Beijing air pollution, London fog (historical)
terms.funFact: The Great Smog of London in 1952 killed about 12,000 people in just 5 days - it led to the world's first clean air laws!

terms.name: Snow
terms.hook: Frozen crystals floating down from winter skies
terms.definition: Precipitation in the form of ice crystals that form in clouds and fall to Earth. Snow crystals have hexagonal symmetry but form in endless unique patterns depending on temperature and humidity conditions.
terms.formation: Ice crystal growth in clouds below 0°C
terms.example: Snowfall, blizzards, lake-effect snow
terms.funFact: No two snowflakes are exactly alike - but they all have six sides because of how water molecules bond!

terms.name: Snowline
terms.hook: The elevation where snow persists year-round
terms.definition: The lowest elevation at which snow remains on the ground throughout the year. The snowline varies with latitude (lower near poles) and local climate factors like precipitation and temperature.
terms.example: Alps snowline (~3,000m), equatorial snowline (~5,000m), Arctic (sea level)
terms.funFact: The snowline is rising worldwide due to climate change - by about 150 meters per century in some mountain ranges!

terms.name: Soil
terms.hook: The living skin of Earth supporting all terrestrial life
terms.definition: The upper layer of Earth's surface consisting of minerals, organic matter, water, air, and living organisms. Soil develops over thousands of years through weathering of rock and decomposition of organic material.
terms.example: Topsoil, subsoil, clay soil, sandy soil, loam
terms.funFact: A single tablespoon of healthy soil contains more organisms than there are people on Earth!

terms.name: Soil Erosion
terms.hook: Precious topsoil lost to wind and water
terms.definition: The removal of topsoil by water, wind, or tillage faster than it can be naturally replaced. Soil erosion is accelerated by deforestation, overgrazing, and poor agricultural practices, threatening food security.
terms.example: Gully erosion, sheet erosion, wind erosion, Dust Bowl
terms.funFact: It takes about 500 years to form 2.5 cm of topsoil, but erosion can remove that much in a single storm!

terms.name: Soil Horizon
terms.hook: Distinct layers telling the story of soil formation
terms.definition: A layer of soil with distinct characteristics that developed through soil formation processes. Horizons are designated O (organic), A (topsoil), E (eluviated), B (subsoil), C (parent material), and R (bedrock).
terms.example: O horizon (leaf litter), A horizon (dark topsoil), B horizon (clay-rich)
terms.funFact: Soil scientists dig pits called 'pedons' to study soil horizons - each horizon tells part of the soil's story!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'horizōn' (bounding) applied to soil layers

terms.name: Solar Eclipse
terms.hook: The Moon briefly blocking the Sun from view
terms.definition: An eclipse that occurs when the Moon passes between the Sun and Earth, casting a shadow on Earth. During a total solar eclipse, the Sun's corona becomes visible. Solar eclipses occur 2-5 times per year.
terms.location: Visible from specific locations on Earth
terms.formation: Moon passing between Earth and Sun
terms.example: Total solar eclipse (totality), partial eclipse, annular eclipse
terms.funFact: Total solar eclipses are so rare at any given location that the average wait time is 375 years!

terms.name: Solar System
terms.hook: Our cosmic neighborhood bound by the Sun's gravity
terms.definition: The gravitationally bound system comprising the Sun, eight planets, their moons, dwarf planets, asteroids, comets, and other objects. The solar system extends to the Oort Cloud, about 2 light-years from the Sun.
terms.location: Orion Arm of Milky Way galaxy
terms.formation: 4.6 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Sun, planets (Mercury-Neptune), moons, asteroid belt, Kuiper Belt
terms.funFact: If the Sun were the size of a basketball, Earth would be a pea 26 meters away, and Pluto would be 1.6 km distant!

terms.name: Solar Wind
terms.hook: A constant stream of charged particles flowing from the Sun
terms.definition: A stream of charged particles (mostly protons and electrons) released from the Sun's corona, traveling at 400-800 km/s. Solar wind interacts with planetary magnetic fields to create auroras.
terms.example: Aurora formation, comet tails pointing away from Sun
terms.funFact: Solar wind is why comet tails always point away from the Sun - the wind pushes gas and dust outward regardless of travel direction!
terms.etymology: Descriptive term for particle flow from Sun

terms.name: Solifluction
terms.hook: Waterlogged soil flowing slowly downhill in cold regions
terms.definition: The slow, viscous flow of waterlogged soil down slopes in periglacial environments. Solifluction occurs when the surface layer thaws but water cannot drain through the still-frozen soil below.
terms.example: Solifluction lobes, terracettes, stone stripes
terms.funFact: Solifluction can move material only a few centimeters per year, but over thousands of years it shapes entire landscapes!

terms.name: Solstice
terms.hook: The longest and shortest days of the year
terms.definition: Either of the two times per year when the Sun reaches its highest or lowest point in the sky at noon, resulting in the longest and shortest days. Solstices occur around June 21 and December 21.
terms.formation: Earth's orbital position and axial tilt
terms.example: Summer solstice (longest day), winter solstice (shortest day)
terms.funFact: At the summer solstice at the Arctic Circle, the Sun never sets - it's the 'midnight sun'!

terms.name: Sound (Water Body)
terms.hook: Wide ocean passages between mainland and islands
terms.definition: A large sea inlet wider than a strait, or a narrow sea between a mainland and island(s). Sounds can form through tectonic activity, glacial erosion, or sea level changes.
terms.formation: Various - tectonic, glacial, sea level change
terms.example: Puget Sound, Long Island Sound, Plymouth Sound
terms.funFact: Puget Sound was carved by glaciers during the last ice age and has over 2,500 km of shoreline!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'sund' meaning 'sea, water, swimming'

terms.name: River Source
terms.hook: Where rivers begin their journey to the sea
terms.definition: The point where a river originates, typically from springs, glacial meltwater, lakes, or accumulated precipitation in headwater regions. River sources are usually in highlands or mountains.
terms.example: Mississippi source (Lake Itasca), Nile source (Lake Victoria), Amazon source
terms.funFact: The true source of the Nile was debated for centuries - the quest to find it was one of history's great exploration challenges!
terms.etymology: From Old French 'sourse', from Latin 'surgere' (to rise)

terms.name: South Pole
terms.hook: The bottom of the world on an icy continent
terms.definition: The southernmost point on Earth, located on the Antarctic continent at 90°S latitude. Unlike the North Pole, the South Pole sits on land (covered by 2,700 meters of ice) at an elevation of 2,835 meters.
terms.example: Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station, ceremonial pole marker
terms.funFact: The South Pole is actually moving - the ice sheet it sits on flows about 10 meters per year toward the coast!

terms.name: Outer Space
terms.hook: The vast emptiness beyond Earth's atmosphere
terms.definition: The region beyond Earth's atmosphere, extending throughout the universe. Space is a near-perfect vacuum, though it contains low densities of particles, radiation, and magnetic fields.
terms.example: Earth orbit, interplanetary space, interstellar space, intergalactic space
terms.funFact: Space is not completely empty - there's about 1 atom per cubic centimeter in interstellar space, mostly hydrogen!

terms.name: Spit
terms.hook: Sandy fingers reaching out from the shore
terms.definition: A narrow, elongated landform of sand or shingle extending from the coast into open water, formed by longshore drift. Spits often have curved ends (hooks) and may eventually connect to nearby islands, forming tombolos.
terms.example: Spurn Head (England), Cape Cod (Massachusetts), Farewell Spit (New Zealand)
terms.funFact: Spits can grow several meters per year during active sediment transport - they're constantly changing shape!

terms.name: Spring (Water)
terms.hook: Groundwater bursting forth from the Earth
terms.definition: A natural discharge of groundwater at the land surface where the water table intersects the ground. Springs can be permanent or intermittent and range from tiny seeps to major water sources.
terms.example: Hot springs, artesian springs, karst springs
terms.funFact: Florida's springs discharge over 8 billion gallons of water per day - they're some of the largest springs in the world!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'springan' meaning 'to leap forth'

terms.name: Spring Tide
terms.hook: When Sun and Moon align to create extreme tides
terms.definition: A tide of greater-than-average range occurring when the Sun and Moon are aligned (new and full moons), combining their gravitational pull. Spring tides produce the highest high tides and lowest low tides.
terms.example: New moon tides, full moon tides, king tides
terms.funFact: The Bay of Fundy's spring tides can reach 16 meters - tall enough to cover a 5-story building!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'springan' (to rise, swell) - not related to the season

terms.name: Stalactite
terms.hook: Stone icicles hanging from cave ceilings
terms.definition: A mineral formation hanging from the ceiling of a cave, formed by precipitation of minerals from dripping water. Stalactites grow downward at rates of about 0.1-0.5 mm per year.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of mineral precipitation
terms.example: Carlsbad Caverns, Waitomo Caves, Mammoth Cave
terms.funFact: Memory trick: stalactites hold 'tight' to the ceiling, stalagmites 'might' reach the ceiling someday!

terms.name: Stalagmite
terms.hook: Stone pillars rising from cave floors
terms.definition: A mineral formation rising from the floor of a cave, formed by precipitation of minerals from water dripping from above. Stalagmites often form beneath stalactites and may eventually join to form columns.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of mineral precipitation
terms.example: Carlsbad Caverns, Luray Caverns, Postojna Cave
terms.funFact: When a stalactite and stalagmite meet, they form a column - but this can take hundreds of thousands of years!

terms.name: Star
terms.hook: Massive balls of burning gas lighting up the universe
terms.definition: A massive, luminous sphere of plasma held together by gravity, generating energy through nuclear fusion. Stars are the fundamental building blocks of galaxies and produce all elements heavier than hydrogen and helium.
terms.example: Sun, Sirius, Betelgeuse, Proxima Centauri
terms.funFact: There are more stars in the universe than grains of sand on all Earth's beaches - about 200 billion trillion (2×10²³)!

terms.name: Steppe
terms.hook: Vast treeless plains of central Eurasia
terms.definition: A semi-arid grassland characterized by seasonal temperature extremes and short grasses. Steppes stretch across central Eurasia and parts of North America, historically home to nomadic peoples.
terms.formation: Climate with hot summers, cold winters, low rainfall
terms.example: Eurasian Steppe, Kazakh Steppe, Pontic-Caspian steppe
terms.funFact: The Eurasian Steppe was history's 'highway' - it enabled the Mongol conquests and spread of horses and technology across continents!

terms.name: Storm
terms.hook: Nature's violent atmospheric outbursts
terms.definition: A disturbance of the atmosphere marked by wind, rain, snow, sleet, hail, lightning, or blowing sand. Storms result from contrast between warm and cold air masses or from deep convection in tropical regions.
terms.example: Thunderstorm, hurricane, blizzard, tornado, dust storm
terms.funFact: A single thunderstorm can produce as much electricity as a nuclear power plant generates in a day!

terms.name: Storm Surge
terms.hook: Walls of water pushed ashore by hurricanes
terms.definition: An abnormal rise in sea level generated by a storm's winds pushing water toward shore. Storm surge is the deadliest aspect of hurricanes, responsible for most fatalities through drowning and destruction.
terms.formation: Wind stress and low pressure raising water level
terms.example: Hurricane Katrina surge (8.5m), Typhoon Haiyan, Galveston 1900
terms.funFact: The Galveston Hurricane of 1900 produced a storm surge that killed 8,000 people - still the deadliest natural disaster in US history!

terms.name: Strait
terms.hook: Narrow water passages connecting larger seas
terms.definition: A narrow passage of water connecting two larger bodies of water. Straits are often strategically important for navigation and can be sites of strong currents due to water flowing between the connected bodies.
terms.formation: Tectonic activity or sea level rise
terms.example: Strait of Gibraltar, Bering Strait, Strait of Magellan, Malacca Strait
terms.funFact: The Strait of Gibraltar is only 14 km wide at its narrowest - you can see Africa from Europe on a clear day!

terms.name: Strata
terms.hook: Rock layers stacked like pages in a history book
terms.definition: Layers or beds of sedimentary rock or soil with internally consistent characteristics. Each stratum represents a distinct period of deposition and can contain fossils and evidence of past environments.
terms.example: Grand Canyon strata, White Cliffs of Dover, varved sediments
terms.funFact: The principle of superposition says the bottom stratum is oldest and the top is youngest - geology's most fundamental law!

terms.name: Stratosphere
terms.hook: The calm layer where jets cruise and ozone protects
terms.definition: The layer of atmosphere above the troposphere, extending from about 12 to 50 km altitude. The stratosphere contains the ozone layer, has increasing temperature with altitude, and is very stable with little weather.
terms.example: Ozone layer, jet aircraft cruising altitude
terms.funFact: Volcanic eruptions can inject ash into the stratosphere, where it can circle the globe for years and cool the climate!

terms.name: Stratus Cloud
terms.hook: Low gray sheets blanketing the sky
terms.definition: Low-level clouds forming in horizontal layers with a relatively flat base and top. Stratus clouds often cover the entire sky and can produce drizzle or light precipitation. They form when stable air is lifted gently.
terms.formation: Gentle lifting of stable, moist air
terms.example: Fog (stratus at ground level), overcast days
terms.funFact: When stratus clouds touch the ground, we call them fog - they're essentially the same thing at different elevations!

terms.name: Stream
terms.hook: Small flowing waters feeding the rivers
terms.definition: A body of flowing water smaller than a river, moving within a channel toward lower elevations. Streams form the upper portions of drainage networks and can be perennial, intermittent, or ephemeral.
terms.example: Mountain streams, brooks, creeks
terms.funFact: All rivers start as small streams - even the mighty Amazon begins as tiny trickles of water high in the Andes!

terms.name: Glacial Striation
terms.hook: Scratches carved in rock by glaciers dragging stones
terms.definition: Parallel grooves, scratches, or lines on bedrock surfaces caused by rocks embedded in the base of a glacier scraping across the surface. Striations indicate the direction of past ice flow.
terms.example: Central Park striations, Scottish Highlands, Alpine valleys
terms.funFact: Scientists can determine which direction an ancient glacier moved by examining striations - they're like arrows pointing the way!

terms.name: Subduction
terms.hook: Ocean plates diving beneath continents into Earth's depths
terms.definition: The process by which one tectonic plate moves under another and descends into the mantle. Subduction zones are marked by ocean trenches, volcanic arcs, and the most powerful earthquakes on Earth.
terms.example: Cascadia Subduction Zone, Japan Trench, Peru-Chile Trench
terms.funFact: Subducting plates can reach depths of 700 km - but they're still cold enough that earthquakes occur even at those depths!
terms.etymology: From Latin 'subducere' meaning 'to draw from below'

terms.name: Sublimation
terms.hook: Ice vanishing directly into vapor without melting
terms.definition: The phase transition of water (or other substances) directly from solid to gas without passing through the liquid phase. Sublimation occurs when ice is exposed to dry air with low vapor pressure.
terms.example: Dry ice evaporating, glacier sublimation, freeze-dried food
terms.funFact: In Antarctica, more snow is lost to sublimation than to melting - the air is so dry that ice evaporates directly!

terms.name: Submarine Canyon
terms.hook: Grand canyons carved into the ocean floor
terms.definition: A steep-sided valley cut into the continental shelf and slope, often originating near river mouths. Submarine canyons are carved by turbidity currents - underwater avalanches of sediment-laden water.
terms.example: Monterey Canyon, Hudson Canyon, Congo Canyon
terms.funFact: The Monterey Canyon is deeper and larger than the Grand Canyon - but it's hidden beneath the waves of California!

terms.name: Suburb
terms.hook: Residential areas spreading around city cores
terms.definition: A residential area outside the central part of a city, typically characterized by lower density, single-family homes, and automobile dependence. Suburbs grew rapidly after World War II with increased car ownership.
terms.example: American suburbs, British commuter towns, edge cities
terms.funFact: The average American suburb dweller drives about 15,000 km per year - three times more than city center residents!

terms.name: Ecological Succession
terms.hook: The gradual transformation of ecosystems over time
terms.definition: The process of change in species composition and community structure over time following a disturbance or new land creation. Primary succession occurs on new surfaces; secondary succession follows disturbance of existing ecosystems.
terms.example: Forest regrowth after fire, pond filling with vegetation, volcanic island colonization
terms.funFact: After Krakatau erupted in 1883, scientists have watched succession transform bare volcanic rock into tropical forest over 140 years!

terms.name: Sun
terms.hook: The star that makes all life on Earth possible
terms.definition: The star at the center of our solar system, containing 99.86% of the system's total mass. The Sun is a G-type main-sequence star, about 4.6 billion years old, and will continue burning for another 5 billion years.
terms.formation: 4.6 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Solar flares, sunspots, corona, solar wind
terms.funFact: The Sun converts 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium every second - that's the source of all its energy!

terms.name: Supernova
terms.hook: Stellar explosions briefly outshining entire galaxies
terms.definition: A powerful and luminous explosion occurring at the end of some stars' lives, either from core collapse of massive stars or thermonuclear explosion of white dwarfs. Supernovae spread heavy elements throughout the universe.
terms.example: SN 1987A, Crab Nebula supernova (1054 AD), Tycho's supernova (1572)
terms.funFact: Every heavy element in your body - carbon, oxygen, iron, gold - was created in supernovae. You are literally made of exploded stars!

terms.name: Supervolcano
terms.hook: Volcanic systems capable of world-changing eruptions
terms.definition: A volcanic center that has produced an eruption of magnitude 8 or higher on the Volcanic Explosivity Index, ejecting more than 1,000 km³ of material. Supervolcano eruptions can cause global climate disruption.
terms.example: Yellowstone, Toba, Long Valley Caldera, Taupo
terms.funFact: The last Yellowstone supereruption (640,000 years ago) ejected enough material to bury all of New York State under 1 meter of ash!

terms.name: Sustainable Development
terms.hook: Meeting today's needs without compromising tomorrow
terms.definition: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs. Sustainable development balances economic growth, environmental protection, and social equity.
terms.example: Renewable energy, green buildings, sustainable agriculture
terms.funFact: The UN's 17 Sustainable Development Goals were adopted by 193 countries in 2015 - the most ambitious global plan ever agreed upon!

terms.name: Swamp
terms.hook: Waterlogged forests where trees stand in water
terms.definition: A forested wetland with permanently or seasonally saturated ground and standing water, dominated by woody plants (trees and shrubs). Swamps differ from marshes, which are dominated by herbaceous plants.
terms.example: Everglades, Louisiana swamps, Pantanal, Okavango
terms.funFact: The Everglades is actually a slow-moving river 100 km wide and only 15 cm deep - it flows at just 0.5 km per day!

terms.name: Ocean Swell
terms.hook: Long-distance waves traveling across entire oceans
terms.definition: Long-period waves that have traveled out of the area where they were generated by wind. Swells can travel thousands of kilometers across oceans with minimal energy loss, eventually breaking as surf on distant shores.
terms.example: Pacific swells reaching Hawaii, Atlantic swells in Europe
terms.funFact: Swells generated by storms in Antarctica can travel over 10,000 km to crash on beaches in California!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'swellan' meaning 'to grow, become strong'

terms.name: Syncline
terms.hook: Rock layers folded downward like a trough
terms.definition: A fold in rock layers where the youngest rocks are in the center and older rocks are on the outside - the opposite of an anticline. Synclines form concave-upward structures from compressional forces.
terms.example: Valley and ridge folds (Appalachians), Welsh synclines
terms.funFact: Synclines often form valleys because the folded rocks in the center are more easily eroded than the surrounding anticlines!

terms.name: Taiga
terms.hook: Earth's largest land biome stretching across the north
terms.definition: The boreal forest biome characterized by coniferous forests of spruce, pine, and fir, stretching across subarctic regions. Taiga has long, cold winters and short, warm summers, and stores vast amounts of carbon.
terms.example: Siberian taiga, Canadian boreal forest, Scandinavian taiga
terms.funFact: The taiga contains more trees than all other biomes combined - about 1.2 trillion trees!

terms.name: Talus
terms.hook: Piles of broken rock at the base of cliffs
terms.definition: An accumulation of angular rock debris at the base of a cliff or steep slope, formed by rockfall and weathering. Talus slopes are characteristically steep, at the angle of repose for the material.
terms.location: Base of cliffs and steep slopes
terms.example: Alpine talus slopes, canyon walls, cliff bases
terms.funFact: Talus slopes are at the 'angle of repose' - about 34-36° - add one more rock and they avalanche down!

terms.name: Tarn
terms.hook: Crystal lakes cradled in glacial mountain bowls
terms.definition: A mountain lake formed in a cirque excavated by glacial erosion. Tarns are typically small, deep, and circular, occupying the basin left when the glacier that carved the cirque melted away.
terms.example: Lake Helene (Rocky Mountains), Welsh tarns, Scottish corrie lochs
terms.funFact: The Lake District in England has so many tarns that 'tarn' became the local word for any small mountain lake!

terms.name: Tectonic Plate
terms.hook: Giant puzzle pieces that make up Earth's surface
terms.definition: A massive segment of Earth's lithosphere that moves, floats, and sometimes fractures, interacting with other plates at boundaries. Earth has about 15 major plates and many smaller ones.
terms.example: Pacific Plate (largest), North American Plate, Eurasian Plate
terms.funFact: The Pacific Plate moves about 10 cm per year - that's as fast as your fingernails grow!

terms.name: Temperate Climate
terms.hook: Moderate climates between tropical heat and polar cold
terms.definition: A climate zone between the tropics and polar regions, characterized by moderate temperatures and distinct seasons. Temperate climates include Mediterranean, oceanic, continental, and humid subtropical types.
terms.example: Western Europe, eastern United States, southern Australia, Japan
terms.funFact: Temperate regions produce most of the world's food because their moderate climates and seasonal rainfall are ideal for agriculture!

terms.name: Temperature
terms.hook: The measure of heat energy in the environment
terms.definition: A measure of the average kinetic energy of particles in a substance. In meteorology and climatology, temperature describes atmospheric warmth and is measured in Celsius, Fahrenheit, or Kelvin.
terms.example: Air temperature, sea surface temperature, soil temperature
terms.funFact: The highest temperature ever recorded was 56.7°C in Death Valley (1913), and the lowest was -89.2°C in Antarctica (1983)!

terms.name: Terminal Moraine
terms.hook: Ridges marking where glaciers reached their farthest point
terms.definition: A ridge of glacial debris deposited at the farthest advance of a glacier. Terminal moraines mark the maximum extent of glaciation and form distinctive linear hills across landscapes.
terms.example: Long Island, Cape Cod, Alpine terminal moraines
terms.funFact: Long Island is essentially a giant terminal moraine - a pile of debris left by the last ice age glacier about 20,000 years ago!

terms.name: Agricultural Terrace
terms.hook: Stairstepped fields carved into mountainsides
terms.definition: A leveled section of a hilly area developed for farming, creating a series of successively receding flat surfaces. Terracing prevents soil erosion and allows cultivation on steep slopes.
terms.example: Banaue Rice Terraces (Philippines), Peruvian Andes, Chinese rice terraces
terms.funFact: The Banaue Rice Terraces in the Philippines were carved by hand over 2,000 years ago - they're sometimes called the 'Eighth Wonder of the World'!

terms.name: Terrestrial
terms.hook: Everything that lives and happens on land
terms.definition: Relating to or occurring on land, as opposed to aquatic (water) or aerial (air) environments. Terrestrial ecosystems include forests, grasslands, deserts, and tundra.
terms.example: Terrestrial animals, terrestrial plants, terrestrial ecosystems
terms.funFact: Life existed in the oceans for over 3 billion years before the first organisms colonized terrestrial environments about 500 million years ago!

terms.name: Thermocline
terms.hook: The invisible boundary where warm and cold waters meet
terms.definition: A layer in a body of water where temperature changes more rapidly with depth than in layers above or below. The oceanic thermocline separates warm surface water from cold deep water.
terms.example: Oceanic thermocline (200-1000m depth), lake thermoclines
terms.funFact: The thermocline acts as a barrier to mixing - nutrients trapped in cold deep water can't reach the sunlit surface where they're needed!

terms.name: Thermosphere
terms.hook: The hot outer layer where satellites orbit
terms.definition: The layer of atmosphere above the mesosphere, extending from about 85 to 600 km altitude. Despite high temperatures (up to 2,500°C), it would feel cold because particles are so sparse they transfer little heat.
terms.example: Aurora formation zone, International Space Station orbit
terms.funFact: The thermosphere is hot by temperature (2,500°C) but you'd freeze there - there are so few molecules that very little heat would reach you!

terms.name: Thunderstorm
terms.hook: Towering clouds unleashing lightning and downpours
terms.definition: A storm characterized by lightning, thunder, heavy rain, and sometimes hail or tornadoes. Thunderstorms develop from cumulonimbus clouds when warm, moist air rises rapidly, creating strong updrafts and electrical charge separation.
terms.example: Afternoon convection, frontal storms, supercells
terms.funFact: At any moment, there are about 2,000 thunderstorms occurring around the world, producing 100 lightning strikes per second!

terms.name: Tide
terms.hook: The ocean's daily rise and fall pulled by Moon and Sun
terms.definition: The periodic rise and fall of sea level caused by the gravitational attraction of the Moon and Sun acting on Earth's rotating surface. Most coastlines experience two high tides and two low tides daily.
terms.example: High tide, low tide, spring tide, neap tide
terms.funFact: Tides have been gradually slowing Earth's rotation - days were only 6 hours long when life first appeared 3.5 billion years ago!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'tīd' meaning 'time, period, season'

terms.name: Tide Pool
terms.hook: Rocky aquariums left behind by retreating tides
terms.definition: A pool of seawater left in a rocky depression when the tide retreats. Tide pools host specialized organisms that tolerate wide variations in temperature, salinity, and oxygen as waves refresh them.
terms.example: Pacific coast tide pools, New England rocky shores
terms.funFact: Tide pool creatures experience conditions more extreme than any other marine habitat - temperatures can swing 20°C in hours!

terms.name: Glacial Till
terms.hook: Unsorted glacial debris dumped directly by ice
terms.definition: Unsorted sediment deposited directly by glacial ice, containing a mix of particle sizes from clay to boulders. Till differs from water-sorted glacial sediments and is the primary material in moraines.
terms.example: Moraines, drumlins, till plains
terms.funFact: Till can contain rocks from hundreds of kilometers away - geologists use these 'erratics' to trace where glaciers came from!

terms.name: Timberline
terms.hook: Where trees surrender to the harsh alpine heights
terms.definition: The upper elevation limit of tree growth on mountains, above which conditions are too severe for tree survival. Also called treeline, it varies with latitude and local climate factors.
terms.example: Alps (~2,000m), Rockies (~3,500m), tropics (~4,000m)
terms.funFact: At the timberline, trees become twisted and stunted 'krummholz' - German for 'crooked wood' - sculpted by wind and ice!

terms.name: Time Zone
terms.hook: Dividing Earth into hours of local time
terms.definition: A region of the globe that observes a uniform standard time for legal, commercial, and social purposes. Earth is divided into 24 primary time zones, each roughly 15° of longitude wide.
terms.example: Eastern Standard Time, Greenwich Mean Time, China Standard Time
terms.funFact: China spans 5 geographical time zones but uses only one official time - when it's noon in Beijing, it's still dark in western China!

terms.name: Tombolo
terms.hook: Sandy bridges connecting islands to the mainland
terms.definition: A sandbar or spit that connects an island to the mainland or to another island. Tombolos form through wave refraction and sediment deposition in the wave shadow behind an island.
terms.example: Mont Saint-Michel (France), Gibraltar, Chesil Beach (England)
terms.funFact: Mont Saint-Michel was connected to the mainland by a natural tombolo, then a causeway was built, and now engineers are restoring the natural tombolo!

terms.name: Topography
terms.hook: The shape and features of Earth's surface
terms.definition: The arrangement of natural and artificial physical features of an area, including relief (elevation differences), landforms, water bodies, and human-made structures. Topographic maps represent these features.
terms.example: Mountains, valleys, plains, rivers, cities
terms.funFact: The first accurate topographic survey was completed by France in 1789 - it took 55 years and required triangulation from mountaintops!

terms.name: Tor
terms.hook: Craggy rock towers crowning rounded hilltops
terms.definition: A high, rocky hill or a pile of rocks on top of a hill, formed by weathering of jointed bedrock. Tors are characteristic features of granite landscapes like Dartmoor in England.
terms.location: Granite and other resistant rock regions
terms.formation: Millions of years of chemical and physical weathering
terms.example: Dartmoor tors (England), Haytor, Castle Rock
terms.funFact: Dartmoor's tors are sacred sites in Celtic tradition - ancient peoples believed they were formed by giants!
terms.etymology: From Old English 'torr' meaning 'high rock, rocky peak'

terms.name: Tornado
terms.hook: Violently rotating columns of air touching ground
terms.definition: A rapidly rotating column of air extending from a cumulonimbus cloud to the ground, with winds up to 500 km/h. Tornadoes are the most violent storms on Earth and cause intense localized destruction.
terms.formation: Minutes to hours in severe thunderstorms
terms.example: Tornado Alley storms, Joplin 2011, Tri-State Tornado 1925
terms.funFact: The United States experiences about 1,200 tornadoes per year - more than any other country due to its unique geography!

terms.name: Trade Winds
terms.hook: The reliable tropical winds that powered global sailing
terms.definition: Steady easterly winds blowing toward the equator from subtropical high-pressure belts - northeast in the Northern Hemisphere, southeast in the Southern Hemisphere. Trade winds were crucial for historical sailing routes.
terms.example: Caribbean trade winds, Pacific trade winds
terms.funFact: Columbus used trade winds to sail west to the Americas, then westerlies to return to Europe - a brilliant use of global wind patterns!

terms.name: Transform Boundary
terms.hook: Where plates grind past each other sideways
terms.definition: A plate boundary where two tectonic plates slide horizontally past each other without creating or destroying lithosphere. Transform boundaries are marked by strike-slip faults and frequent earthquakes.
terms.example: San Andreas Fault, Alpine Fault (New Zealand), Dead Sea Transform
terms.funFact: The San Andreas Fault moves about 46 mm per year - in about 15 million years, Los Angeles will be next to San Francisco!

terms.name: Transpiration
terms.hook: Plants releasing water vapor through their leaves
terms.definition: The process by which plants release water vapor through stomata (pores) in their leaves. Transpiration moves water and nutrients through plants and returns about 10% of precipitation to the atmosphere.
terms.example: Forest transpiration, crop evapotranspiration
terms.funFact: A single large oak tree can transpire over 400 liters of water per day - that's enough to fill 4 bathtubs!

terms.name: Treeline
terms.hook: The invisible boundary where forests can no longer survive
terms.definition: The edge of the habitat at which trees can grow, either at high altitude (alpine treeline) or high latitude (arctic treeline). Beyond the treeline, conditions are too harsh for tree survival.
terms.example: Alpine treeline, arctic treeline, krummholz zone
terms.funFact: The treeline is rising worldwide due to climate change - trees are colonizing areas that were too cold for them just decades ago!

terms.name: Ocean Trench
terms.hook: The deepest scars on Earth's surface
terms.definition: A long, narrow, steep-sided depression in the ocean floor, typically occurring at subduction zones. Ocean trenches are the deepest parts of the ocean, with the Mariana Trench reaching 10,994 meters depth.
terms.example: Mariana Trench, Peru-Chile Trench, Japan Trench
terms.funFact: If Mount Everest were placed in the Mariana Trench, its peak would still be over 2 km underwater!

terms.name: Tributary
terms.hook: Smaller streams feeding water to larger rivers
terms.definition: A stream or river that flows into a larger stream or river (the main stem). Tributaries contribute water to the drainage network, and their combined discharge forms the main river's flow.
terms.example: Missouri River (Mississippi tributary), Yamuna (Ganges tributary)
terms.funFact: The Amazon has over 1,100 tributaries, 17 of which are over 1,500 km long - longer than most major rivers!

terms.name: Tropical Climate
terms.hook: The warm, wet belt girding Earth's equator
terms.definition: A climate zone near the equator with consistently high temperatures (above 18°C year-round) and abundant rainfall. Tropical climates include rainforest, monsoon, and savanna subtypes.
terms.example: Amazon Basin, Congo Basin, Southeast Asia, Central America
terms.funFact: Tropical rainforests receive so much rain that water rarely reaches the ground - it's intercepted and evaporated by vegetation!

terms.name: Tropic of Cancer
terms.hook: The northern boundary of the Sun's overhead position
terms.definition: The line of latitude at 23.5°N marking the northernmost position where the Sun can appear directly overhead at noon. This occurs on the summer solstice (around June 21).
terms.example: Passes through Mexico, Egypt, India, China
terms.funFact: The Tropic of Cancer got its name 2,000 years ago when the Sun was in the constellation Cancer during the June solstice - it's now in Taurus!

terms.name: Tropic of Capricorn
terms.hook: The southern boundary of the Sun's overhead position
terms.definition: The line of latitude at 23.5°S marking the southernmost position where the Sun can appear directly overhead at noon. This occurs on the December solstice (around December 21).
terms.example: Passes through Australia, South Africa, Brazil, Chile
terms.funFact: The area between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn receives more solar energy than anywhere else on Earth!

terms.name: Troposphere
terms.hook: The weather layer where we live and breathe
terms.definition: The lowest layer of Earth's atmosphere, extending from the surface to about 12 km altitude. The troposphere contains 75% of atmospheric mass and all weather phenomena, with temperature decreasing with altitude.
terms.example: Clouds, storms, winds, precipitation
terms.funFact: All weather happens in the troposphere - even the highest thunderstorm tops barely reach the tropopause at 12 km!

terms.name: Atmospheric Trough
terms.hook: Dips in the jet stream bringing stormy weather
terms.definition: An elongated region of low atmospheric pressure, often extending from a low-pressure center. Troughs are associated with cloudy, unsettled weather and precipitation, and are the opposite of ridges.
terms.example: Cold air troughs, upper-level troughs, jet stream dips
terms.funFact: When a trough passes overhead, expect weather to change dramatically - they're often the trigger for storm development!

terms.name: Tsunami
terms.hook: Massive waves triggered by undersea upheavals
terms.definition: A series of ocean waves with very long wavelengths (up to hundreds of kilometers) caused by large-scale disturbance of the ocean, such as earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, or landslides. Tsunamis can travel at 800 km/h and cause catastrophic coastal flooding.
terms.formation: Seconds to hours after triggering event
terms.example: 2004 Indian Ocean tsunami, 2011 Japan tsunami, 1883 Krakatoa tsunami
terms.funFact: In deep ocean, tsunamis are only about 1 meter high - but they can grow to 30+ meters as they enter shallow water!

terms.name: Tundra
terms.hook: Treeless frozen plains at the top of the world
terms.definition: A cold biome characterized by permafrost, extremely low temperatures, short growing seasons, and lack of trees. Tundra vegetation includes mosses, lichens, sedges, and dwarf shrubs.
terms.formation: Climate too cold for tree growth
terms.example: Arctic tundra, alpine tundra, Antarctic peninsula
terms.funFact: Tundra soils store about 25% of Earth's soil carbon - as permafrost melts, this carbon could accelerate climate change!
terms.etymology: From Kildin Sámi 'tūndâr' meaning 'treeless mountain tract'

terms.name: Turbidity Current
terms.hook: Underwater avalanches carving submarine canyons
terms.definition: A fast-moving underwater current of sediment-laden water that flows down continental slopes and submarine canyons. Turbidity currents can travel at 100 km/h and transport huge volumes of sediment to deep ocean basins.
terms.formation: Earthquake, storm, or sediment accumulation triggers
terms.example: Grand Banks turbidity current (1929), Congo Canyon flows
terms.funFact: The 1929 Grand Banks turbidity current traveled 600 km across the ocean floor, cutting submarine telegraph cables one by one as it went!

terms.name: Twilight
terms.hook: The magical light between day and night
terms.definition: The period of time before sunrise and after sunset when the Sun is below the horizon but its light illuminates the sky. Twilight is divided into civil, nautical, and astronomical based on the Sun's angle below the horizon.
terms.example: Dawn, dusk, golden hour, blue hour
terms.funFact: At the poles, twilight can last for weeks during the transitions between polar day and polar night!

terms.name: Typhoon
terms.hook: Pacific Ocean's version of the devastating hurricane
terms.definition: A mature tropical cyclone in the western North Pacific Ocean with sustained winds of 119 km/h or greater. Typhoons are the same phenomenon as hurricanes but are named differently based on location.
terms.example: Typhoon Tip (1979, largest), Typhoon Haiyan (2013), Typhoon Mangkhut (2018)
terms.funFact: Typhoon Tip in 1979 was the largest tropical cyclone ever recorded, with a diameter of 2,220 km - half the width of the continental US!

terms.name: U-Shaped Valley
terms.hook: Valleys scooped out by glaciers into broad troughs
terms.definition: A valley with a characteristic U-shaped cross-section, carved by glacial erosion. U-shaped valleys have steep sides and flat floors, contrasting with V-shaped valleys carved by rivers.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of glacial erosion
terms.example: Yosemite Valley, Norwegian fjords, Swiss Alps valleys
terms.funFact: Yosemite Valley was carved by glaciers at least 1,000 meters thick - the massive cliffs like El Capitan are what remains!

terms.name: Ultraviolet Radiation
terms.hook: Invisible solar rays that tan, burn, and power life
terms.definition: Electromagnetic radiation with wavelengths shorter than visible light (10-400 nm). UV radiation from the Sun is partially absorbed by the ozone layer; the portion reaching Earth can damage cells but also enables vitamin D production.
terms.example: UV-A (tanning), UV-B (sunburn), UV-C (absorbed by ozone)
terms.funFact: Life couldn't exist on land until the ozone layer formed 600 million years ago to block lethal UV radiation!

terms.name: Unconformity
terms.hook: Gaps in the rock record where time is missing
terms.definition: A buried erosional or non-depositional surface separating two rock masses of different ages, indicating a gap in the geological record. Unconformities represent periods when rocks were eroded or deposition stopped.
terms.example: Great Unconformity (Grand Canyon), angular unconformity
terms.funFact: The Great Unconformity in the Grand Canyon represents a gap of 1.2 billion years - over a quarter of Earth's history is simply missing!

terms.name: Understory
terms.hook: The shaded forest layer beneath the canopy
terms.definition: The layer of vegetation between the forest canopy and the ground, consisting of shrubs, small trees, and herbaceous plants. The understory receives filtered light and creates distinct habitat conditions.
terms.example: Rainforest understory, temperate forest shrub layer
terms.funFact: Rainforest understories are so dark that only about 2% of sunlight reaches the forest floor - plants there have special adaptations!

terms.name: Upland
terms.hook: Higher terrain rising above surrounding lowlands
terms.definition: An area of relatively high ground, such as hills, plateaus, or mountain foothills. Uplands typically have different climate, vegetation, and land use than adjacent lowlands.
terms.example: Scottish Uplands, Appalachian uplands, African plateau
terms.funFact: Uplands often receive more rainfall than lowlands because air is forced to rise - they're natural water towers for regions below!

terms.name: Tectonic Uplift
terms.hook: Earth's crust rising under tectonic pressure
terms.definition: The upward movement of Earth's surface due to tectonic forces such as plate collision, volcanic activity, or isostatic rebound. Uplift creates mountains and raises formerly submerged rocks above sea level.
terms.formation: Millions of years of tectonic activity
terms.example: Himalayan uplift, Rocky Mountain uplift, post-glacial rebound
terms.funFact: The Himalayas are still rising about 5 mm per year - but erosion nearly keeps pace, so they grow very slowly!

terms.name: Upwelling
terms.hook: Cold nutrient-rich water rising from the deep
terms.definition: An oceanographic phenomenon where deep, cold, nutrient-rich water rises to the surface. Upwelling zones are among the most productive marine ecosystems, supporting large fisheries and diverse marine life.
terms.example: Peru Current upwelling, California Current, Benguela Current
terms.funFact: Upwelling zones make up only 0.1% of ocean area but produce 50% of the world's fish catch!

terms.name: Urban
terms.hook: The human-dominated landscapes of cities
terms.definition: Relating to towns and cities, characterized by high population density, extensive built environment, and economic activities dominated by manufacturing and services rather than agriculture.
terms.example: Urban core, urban sprawl, urbanization
terms.funFact: In 2007, for the first time in human history, more people lived in urban areas than rural areas - we're now an urban species!

terms.name: Urbanization
terms.hook: The global trend of people moving to cities
terms.definition: The process by which increasing proportions of a population come to live in urban areas, and the growth and spread of urban environments. Urbanization involves physical expansion of cities and social/economic transformation.
terms.formation: Decades to centuries of migration and growth
terms.example: Chinese urbanization, African urbanization, megacity growth
terms.funFact: By 2050, 68% of the world's population will live in cities - that's 6.7 billion urban dwellers!

terms.name: Uranus
terms.hook: The ice giant rolling sideways through space
terms.definition: The seventh planet from the Sun, an ice giant with a unique tilted axis (98°) causing extreme seasonal variations. Uranus has 27 known moons and a ring system, and appears blue-green due to methane in its atmosphere.
terms.formation: 4.5 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Moons (Miranda, Ariel), ring system, extreme axial tilt
terms.funFact: Uranus rotates on its side - scientists think it was knocked over by an Earth-sized object billions of years ago!
terms.etymology: Named after Greek god of the sky

terms.name: V-Shaped Valley
terms.hook: Valleys carved by rivers into narrow gorges
terms.definition: A valley with a V-shaped cross-section, carved primarily by stream erosion. V-shaped valleys form in non-glaciated regions where streams cut downward faster than slopes can widen, creating steep-sided gorges.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of river erosion
terms.example: Grand Canyon, river gorges, mountain stream valleys
terms.funFact: Rivers carve V-shaped valleys by cutting down into rock while weathering widens the slopes - it's a race between two processes!

terms.name: Valley
terms.hook: Low areas cradled between higher ground
terms.definition: A low area of land between hills or mountains, typically with a river flowing through it. Valleys form through erosion by rivers or glaciers, or through tectonic activity creating grabens.
terms.example: Yosemite Valley, Death Valley, Nile Valley
terms.funFact: Death Valley holds the record for the lowest point in North America at 86 meters below sea level - and the hottest temperature ever recorded!

terms.name: Van Allen Radiation Belts
terms.hook: Invisible rings of radiation trapped by Earth's field
terms.definition: Two doughnut-shaped zones of energetic charged particles trapped by Earth's magnetic field. The belts extend from about 1,000 to 60,000 km above Earth and pose radiation hazards for spacecraft and astronauts.
terms.formation: Solar wind particles trapped by magnetic field
terms.example: Inner belt (protons), outer belt (electrons)
terms.funFact: Apollo astronauts passed through the Van Allen belts quickly to minimize radiation exposure - they received less radiation than a CT scan!
terms.etymology: Named after James Van Allen who discovered them in 1958

terms.name: Varve
terms.hook: Annual layers in lake sediments counting time
terms.definition: A pair of contrasting laminae deposited in a glacial lake over one year: a lighter, coarser summer layer and a darker, finer winter layer. Varves can be counted like tree rings to date climate events.
terms.example: Lake Van varves, Swedish varves, Green River Formation
terms.funFact: Swedish scientists have counted over 13,000 years of varves, creating a timeline more accurate than radiocarbon dating for that period!

terms.name: Vegetation
terms.hook: The green blanket covering Earth's lands
terms.definition: The plant life of a region, considered as a whole. Vegetation types are classified by structure (forest, grassland, desert) and are determined primarily by climate, soil, and disturbance regimes.
terms.example: Forest, grassland, tundra, desert scrub
terms.funFact: Vegetation produces all the oxygen we breathe and removes CO2 from the atmosphere - it's Earth's life support system!

terms.name: Veld
terms.hook: Southern Africa's open grassland plateaus
terms.definition: A type of open rural landscape in southern Africa, particularly the elevated grasslands of South Africa. The veld varies from tropical savanna (bushveld) to temperate grassland (highveld).
terms.example: Highveld, lowveld, bushveld
terms.funFact: The Highveld of South Africa sits at 1,500 meters elevation - Johannesburg is one of the highest major cities in the world!

terms.name: Volcanic Vent
terms.hook: Openings where volcanoes exhale fire and ash
terms.definition: An opening at Earth's surface through which volcanic material is erupted. Vents can be central (main crater), fissure (long cracks), or flank (side eruptions), and vary from explosive to effusive.
terms.example: Summit crater, flank vents, fissure eruptions
terms.funFact: Kilauea in Hawaii has over 50 vents along its East Rift Zone - it's one of the most active volcanic systems on Earth!

terms.name: Venus
terms.hook: Earth's evil twin shrouded in acid clouds
terms.definition: The second planet from the Sun, similar in size to Earth but with a thick atmosphere of carbon dioxide and sulfuric acid clouds. Venus has the hottest surface of any planet (465°C) due to extreme greenhouse effect.
terms.formation: 4.5 billion years ago from solar nebula
terms.example: Extreme greenhouse effect, volcanic surface, retrograde rotation
terms.funFact: A day on Venus is longer than its year - it rotates so slowly (243 Earth days) and orbits so fast (225 Earth days) that the Sun rises twice per Venus year!
terms.etymology: Named after Roman goddess of love and beauty

terms.name: Vernal Pool
terms.hook: Temporary ponds that burst with life in spring
terms.definition: A seasonal pool of water that provides habitat for distinctive plants and animals. Vernal pools fill with rain and snowmelt in spring, then dry up in summer, supporting specialized organisms adapted to this cycle.
terms.example: California vernal pools, northeastern US vernal pools
terms.funFact: Fairy shrimp evolved specifically for vernal pools - their eggs can survive decades of drought waiting for the pool to fill again!

terms.name: Visibility
terms.hook: How far we can see through the atmosphere
terms.definition: The distance at which objects can be clearly seen through the atmosphere. Visibility is reduced by fog, precipitation, dust, smoke, or other particles in the air, and is critical for aviation and transportation.
terms.example: Clear day (30+ km), fog (under 1 km), haze
terms.funFact: On exceptionally clear days, you can see Mount Everest from over 340 km away - visibility depends on air clarity, not just distance!

terms.name: Volcanic Arc
terms.hook: Curved chains of volcanoes marking subduction zones
terms.definition: A curved chain of volcanoes formed above a subducting oceanic plate, parallel to and inland from an oceanic trench. Volcanic arcs can be oceanic (island arcs) or continental (mountain ranges with volcanoes).
terms.formation: Continuous volcanic activity over millions of years
terms.example: Cascades, Andes, Aleutians, Japan
terms.funFact: The Ring of Fire's volcanic arcs contain 75% of the world's active volcanoes - they're nature's most powerful geological factories!

terms.name: Volcanic Ash
terms.hook: Pulverized rock blasted skyward by eruptions
terms.definition: Fine particles of rock, minerals, and volcanic glass ejected during explosive volcanic eruptions. Volcanic ash can travel thousands of kilometers, disrupt aviation, affect climate, and create fertile soils over time.
terms.example: Mount St. Helens ash, Eyjafjallajökull ash cloud
terms.funFact: The 2010 Eyjafjallajökull eruption grounded 100,000 flights in Europe - volcanic ash can destroy jet engines!

terms.name: Volcano
terms.hook: Mountains of fire connecting Earth's interior to its surface
terms.definition: A vent in Earth's crust through which magma, volcanic ash, and gases escape from below the surface. Volcanoes can be active, dormant, or extinct, and take various forms including shield, stratovolcano, and cinder cone.
terms.formation: Continuous over thousands to millions of years
terms.example: Mount Fuji, Mount Vesuvius, Kilauea, Mount Rainier
terms.funFact: There are about 1,500 potentially active volcanoes on land and perhaps 10 times more underwater - Earth is very volcanic!
terms.etymology: From Italian 'vulcano', from Latin 'Vulcanus' (Roman fire god)

terms.name: Wadi
terms.hook: Desert valleys that flood briefly then run dry
terms.definition: A valley in desert regions that remains dry except during rainy season. Wadis are cut by intermittent streams and can experience dangerous flash floods when rain falls in their catchments.
terms.location: North Africa, Middle East, arid regions
terms.example: Wadi Rum (Jordan), Wadis of the Sahara
terms.funFact: Wadi Rum in Jordan was used as a stand-in for Mars in the movie 'The Martian' - its red sandstone looks otherworldly!

terms.name: Warm Front
terms.hook: Where warm air gently slides over retreating cold air
terms.definition: The boundary where a warm air mass advances and rises over a retreating cold air mass. Warm fronts bring gradual cloud build-up and prolonged, steady precipitation over a wide area.
terms.example: Approaching warm fronts with cirrus clouds, then rain
terms.funFact: Warm fronts move about half as fast as cold fronts because warm air has to gently ride up over the dense cold air!

terms.name: Water Cycle
terms.hook: Water's endless journey through Earth's systems
terms.definition: The continuous movement of water within Earth and atmosphere through evaporation, transpiration, condensation, precipitation, and runoff. The water cycle distributes heat, shapes landscapes, and sustains all life.
terms.example: Evaporation, clouds, rain, rivers, groundwater
terms.funFact: A water molecule spends on average 9 days in the atmosphere, 2 weeks in rivers, 100 years in lakes, and 3,000 years in the ocean!

terms.name: Water Table
terms.hook: The underground surface where rock becomes saturated
terms.definition: The upper surface of the zone of saturation, below which all pore spaces in soil and rock are filled with water. The water table rises and falls with precipitation and pumping.
terms.example: Wells, springs where water table meets surface
terms.funFact: In some areas, overpumping has caused the water table to drop hundreds of feet, drying up wells that have worked for generations!

terms.name: Waterfall
terms.hook: Rivers leaping over cliffs in spectacular curtains
terms.definition: A place where water flows over a vertical drop or series of steep drops in the course of a stream or river. Waterfalls form where resistant rock overlies softer rock, or at hanging valleys.
terms.example: Angel Falls (979m), Niagara Falls, Victoria Falls, Iguazu Falls
terms.funFact: Angel Falls in Venezuela is so tall (979m) that water turns to mist before reaching the bottom on windy days!

terms.name: Watershed
terms.hook: The land area that funnels water to a single outlet
terms.definition: The area of land that drains all precipitation and surface water to a common outlet. Also called a drainage basin or catchment, watersheds are fundamental units for water resource management.
terms.example: Mississippi watershed, Amazon basin, Chesapeake Bay watershed
terms.funFact: The Amazon watershed covers 7 million km² - an area larger than the entire European Union!

terms.name: Waterspout
terms.hook: Tornadoes dancing across open water
terms.definition: A rotating column of air and water spray over a body of water, essentially a tornado over water. Fair-weather waterspouts form from the surface up; tornadic waterspouts are associated with severe thunderstorms.
terms.location: Over oceans, lakes, and coastal areas
terms.example: Florida Keys waterspouts, Great Lakes waterspouts
terms.funFact: The Florida Keys see more waterspouts than anywhere else in the world - sometimes dozens in a single day!

terms.name: Ocean Wave
terms.hook: Energy traveling across the sea surface
terms.definition: A disturbance on the surface of a body of water that propagates energy from one point to another. Waves are generated by wind, earthquakes, or gravitational attraction and are characterized by height, length, and period.
terms.formation: Wind energy transfer to water surface
terms.example: Wind waves, swell, tsunamis, tides
terms.funFact: Ocean waves don't actually move water forward - water particles move in circles as energy passes through!

terms.name: Wave-Cut Platform
terms.hook: Flat rock shelves carved by pounding surf
terms.definition: A flat, rocky surface left at the base of a retreating cliff, carved by wave erosion. As waves undercut the cliff, it collapses, and the platform extends seaward over time.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of wave erosion
terms.example: Pacific coast platforms, British coast platforms
terms.funFact: Some wave-cut platforms are now raised above sea level, showing that sea levels have changed dramatically over geological time!

terms.name: Weather
terms.hook: The atmosphere's day-to-day mood swings
terms.definition: The state of the atmosphere at a particular place and time with respect to temperature, precipitation, wind, humidity, cloudiness, and atmospheric pressure. Weather is the short-term condition; climate is the long-term average.
terms.example: Sunny, rainy, stormy, cloudy, windy conditions
terms.funFact: Weather prediction was considered impossible until the 1950s when computers became powerful enough to model the atmosphere!

terms.name: Weathering
terms.hook: The slow breakdown of rock into smaller pieces
terms.definition: The breakdown of rocks and minerals at Earth's surface through physical, chemical, and biological processes. Weathering creates soil, releases nutrients, and initiates the transformation of rocks into sediment.
terms.example: Frost wedging, oxidation, root growth, acid dissolution
terms.funFact: Weathering has reduced many of Earth's oldest mountains to gentle hills - the Appalachians were once higher than the Himalayas!

terms.name: Westerlies
terms.hook: The prevailing winds that dominate mid-latitudes
terms.definition: The dominant west-to-east winds found in mid-latitudes (30-60°) in both hemispheres. The westerlies drive weather systems from west to east and are responsible for the climate of western continental coastlines.
terms.example: European weather patterns, North American storms
terms.funFact: The Roaring Forties, Furious Fifties, and Screaming Sixties are names sailors gave to the fierce westerlies in the Southern Ocean!

terms.name: Wetland
terms.hook: Transition zones where land and water embrace
terms.definition: An ecosystem characterized by water-saturated soils and hydrophilic vegetation for all or part of the year. Wetlands include marshes, swamps, bogs, and fens, and provide critical ecosystem services.
terms.example: Everglades, Louisiana bayous, Pantanal, Okavango Delta
terms.funFact: Wetlands store more carbon per hectare than any other ecosystem - draining them releases massive amounts of CO2!

terms.name: White Dwarf
terms.hook: The dense remnant cores of dead Sun-like stars
terms.definition: The hot, dense core left after a low to intermediate-mass star exhausts its nuclear fuel and sheds its outer layers. White dwarfs are about Earth-sized but contain nearly the Sun's mass, slowly cooling over billions of years.
terms.formation: End stage of low-mass stellar evolution
terms.example: Sirius B, Procyon B, future fate of our Sun
terms.funFact: A teaspoon of white dwarf material would weigh about 5 tons - it's incredibly dense but not as extreme as a neutron star!
terms.etymology: Descriptive term from color and size

terms.name: Wilderness
terms.hook: Land untouched by human development
terms.definition: An area of land that has retained its natural character and is essentially undeveloped and unmodified by human activity. Wilderness areas are protected to preserve biodiversity and provide recreational and spiritual values.
terms.example: Alaska wilderness, Amazon interior, Antarctic
terms.funFact: Only about 23% of Earth's land area can still be called wilderness - and it's shrinking by about 3% per decade!

terms.name: Wildfire
terms.hook: Flames sweeping across wild landscapes
terms.definition: An uncontrolled fire burning in wildland areas including forests, grasslands, and brush. Wildfires are natural ecosystem processes in many biomes but can become destructive when they threaten human settlements.
terms.formation: Lightning, human causes, or volcanic activity
terms.example: California wildfires, Australian bushfires, Amazon fires
terms.funFact: Some ecosystems, like California chaparral and Australian eucalyptus forests, actually need fire - seeds won't germinate without heat!

terms.name: Wind
terms.hook: Air in motion from high to low pressure
terms.definition: The movement of air relative to Earth's surface, caused by differences in atmospheric pressure. Wind is described by its direction (where it comes from) and speed, and is a fundamental element of weather and climate.
terms.formation: Pressure differences caused by unequal heating
terms.example: Trade winds, monsoons, sea breezes, hurricanes
terms.funFact: The fastest wind ever recorded was 408 km/h during a tornado in Oklahoma in 1999!

terms.name: Wind Chill
terms.hook: How cold it feels when wind steals your heat
terms.definition: The perceived decrease in air temperature felt by the body due to the flow of air. Wind chill measures the rate of heat loss from exposed skin and is important for assessing cold weather hazards.
terms.example: Wind chill advisory, frostbite danger thresholds
terms.funFact: At -30°C with 50 km/h wind, the wind chill can reach -50°C, causing frostbite on exposed skin in under 5 minutes!

terms.name: Windward
terms.hook: The side facing into the prevailing wind
terms.definition: The direction from which the wind is blowing, or the side of a landmass facing the prevailing wind. Windward slopes typically receive more precipitation due to orographic lifting.
terms.location: Relative to landforms and wind direction
terms.example: Windward side of Hawaii, western slopes of Cascades
terms.funFact: The windward side of Mount Waialeale in Hawaii receives over 10,000 mm of rain per year - one of the wettest spots on Earth!

terms.name: Woodland
terms.hook: Open forests where trees share space with grass
terms.definition: A vegetated area with trees that are widely spaced, allowing light to reach the ground and supporting an understory of shrubs, grasses, or herbaceous plants. Woodlands have 25-60% tree canopy cover.
terms.location: Transition zones between forests and grasslands
terms.example: Oak woodlands, savanna woodland, Mediterranean woodland
terms.funFact: California's oak woodlands support more biodiversity than the state's famous redwood forests!

terms.name: Xenolith
terms.hook: Foreign rocks trapped inside other rocks
terms.definition: A fragment of rock embedded within another rock. In igneous rocks, xenoliths are pieces of surrounding rock that fell into magma but didn't melt, providing samples of rock from deep in Earth's crust or mantle.
terms.formation: Incorporated during magma formation or ascent
terms.example: Mantle xenoliths, crustal xenoliths in kimberlite
terms.funFact: Xenoliths in kimberlite pipes give us our only direct samples of Earth's mantle - they're messengers from 150+ km deep!

terms.name: Xerophyte
terms.hook: Plants that thrive where water is scarce
terms.definition: A plant adapted to survive in an environment with little available water, such as a desert or highly saline environment. Xerophytes have special adaptations like thick waxy coatings, small leaves, deep roots, and water storage.
terms.example: Cacti, succulents, Joshua trees, agave
terms.funFact: Saguaro cacti can store 200 gallons of water after a single rainstorm, sustaining them through years of drought!

terms.name: Yardang
terms.hook: Wind-sculpted ridges aligned like ships in the desert
terms.definition: A streamlined rock ridge carved by wind abrasion in deserts, aligned parallel to the prevailing wind. Yardangs can be meters to kilometers long and have steep windward faces and tapered leeward ends.
terms.formation: Thousands of years of wind erosion
terms.example: Lut Desert (Iran) yardangs, Saharan yardangs, Mars yardangs
terms.funFact: The largest yardangs on Earth are in Iran's Lut Desert - some are 150 meters tall and 40 km long!

terms.name: Zenith
terms.hook: The point in the sky directly overhead
terms.definition: The point on the celestial sphere directly above an observer, at 90° altitude from the horizon. The zenith is the highest point a celestial object can reach in the sky for a given location.
terms.example: Sun at zenith on equator during equinoxes
terms.funFact: The Sun is only directly at your zenith if you live between the Tropics of Cancer and Capricorn - and only twice a year!
terms.etymology: From Arabic 'samt ar-ra's' meaning 'direction of the head'

terms.name: Zephyr
terms.hook: A soft, gentle breeze from the west
terms.definition: A light, gentle breeze, traditionally referring to winds from the west. In Greek mythology, Zephyrus was the god of the west wind, associated with spring and fertility.
terms.example: Spring breezes, gentle sea breezes
terms.funFact: The Zephyr was so associated with pleasant weather that the word became poetic shorthand for any gentle, refreshing breeze!
terms.etymology: From Greek 'Zephyros', god of the west wind

terms.name: Zone of Ablation
terms.hook: Where glaciers lose mass to melting and evaporation
terms.definition: The lower portion of a glacier where mass loss through melting, evaporation, and calving exceeds mass gain from snowfall. The zone of ablation is separated from the accumulation zone by the equilibrium line.
terms.example: Glacier termini, lower valley glaciers
terms.funFact: Climate change is expanding ablation zones on glaciers worldwide - the line where melting exceeds snowfall is moving uphill!

terms.name: Zone of Accumulation
terms.hook: Where glaciers gain mass from falling snow
terms.definition: The upper portion of a glacier where mass gain from snowfall exceeds mass loss through melting. Snow accumulates, compacts into firn, then ice, feeding the glacier's downstream flow.
terms.example: Glacier source areas, high mountain ice fields
terms.funFact: In the accumulation zone, snow compresses into ice over decades - air bubbles trapped inside preserve ancient atmospheric samples!

terms.name: Zooplankton
terms.hook: Tiny drifting animals that form the ocean's food base
terms.definition: Small animals and animal-like organisms that drift in water bodies, unable to swim against currents. Zooplankton includes copepods, krill, jellyfish larvae, and many other organisms that form the base of aquatic food webs.
terms.example: Copepods, krill, jellyfish, larval fish
terms.funFact: Krill (a type of zooplankton) form swarms so massive they can be seen from space - and whales eat up to 4 tons per day!
